STEP 1: HANA VECTOR STORE SCHEMA SETUP 

Creating the REAL_VECTOR Schema in HANA Cloud

What REAL_VECTOR Is

REAL_VECTOR is a native HANA Cloud data type introduced for vector storage. It stores fixed-dimension floating-point arrays — the numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the semantic meaning of text. HANA's vector engine supports cosine similarity and L2 distance calculations directly in SQL, which means your similarity search runs inside the database without moving data out.

REAL_VECTOR is a HANA Cloud feature — it is not available in on-premise SAP HANA. Confirm your HANA Cloud instance version supports vector engine capabilities before proceeding. As of 2026, vector store functionality requires HANA Cloud QRC 1/2024 or later.

Schema Creation

Create a dedicated schema for your vector store tables. Keeping vector store tables separate from operational SAP data makes access control and maintenance significantly cleaner.

-- Create dedicated schema for vector store
CREATE SCHEMA AI_VECTOR_STORE;
 
-- General purpose document embedding table
CREATE TABLE AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (
    EMBEDDING_ID    NVARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    SOURCE_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,  -- e.g. 'KB', 'DDIC', 'TRANSPORT'
    SOURCE_ID       NVARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,  -- original record identifier
    CHUNK_INDEX     INTEGER         NOT NULL,  -- chunk position within source doc
    CHUNK_TEXT      NCLOB           NOT NULL,  -- original text of this chunk
    EMBEDDING       REAL_VECTOR(1536),         -- embedding vector (1536 dims)
    METADATA        NCLOB,                     -- JSON metadata for filtering
    CREATED_AT      TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
 
-- Index for fast source type filtering before vector search
CREATE INDEX IDX_DOC_EMB_SOURCE ON AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID);

The EMBEDDING column dimension (1536) must match the output dimension of your embedding model exactly. The SAP embedding model used in this series outputs 1536-dimensional vectors. If you change embedding models, you must recreate the table with the correct dimension — existing embeddings cannot be reused with a different model.

STEP 2: GENERATING EMBEDDINGS VIA AI CORE 

Generating Embeddings with the SAP Embedding Model

Why the SAP Embedding Model

The SAP embedding model available through AI Core's Generative AI Hub is tuned for enterprise and business content — the kind of domain-specific language that appears in SAP documentation, functional specs, transport descriptions, and master data. For SAP-specific RAG applications, it consistently outperforms general-purpose embedding models on retrieval precision for SAP terminology and process language.

Using it through the Hub also keeps the entire pipeline within the BTP trust boundary — the same governance, audit logging, and data residency controls that apply to your generative AI calls apply to embedding generation too.

Document Chunking Strategy

Chunking — splitting source documents into segments before embedding — is one of the most consequential decisions in a RAG pipeline. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding signal. Chunks that are too small lose context. For SAP content, the following strategy produced the best retrieval results across our four use cases:

 

Source Type

Chunk Size

Chunking Approach

SAP Knowledge Base articles

500–700 tokens per chunk

Paragraph boundaries — keep procedural steps together

HANA data dictionary descriptions

Full field/table description as single chunk

Short content — no chunking needed

Transport / change log entries

One transport record per chunk

Natural boundary — each transport is self-contained

Master data descriptions

One record per chunk with key fields as prefix

Prefix with MATNR/LIFNR for retrieval context

Embedding Generation Code

# Step 2: Generate embeddings using SAP embedding model via AI Core
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json, uuid
 
# Initialize SDK client — credentials from environment or config file
client = OpenAI()
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443, user='<user>', password='<password>', encrypt=True
)
 
def generate_embedding(text: str) -> list:
    """Generate embedding vector using SAP embedding model via AI Core."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-ada-002',
        # SAP-provided embedding model — routed through AI Core
        input=text
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding
 
def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 600, overlap: int = 50) -> list:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks by word count."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks
 
def store_embeddings(source_type: str, source_id: str,
                     text: str, metadata: dict = None):
    """Chunk, embed, and store a document in HANA vector store."""
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        embedding = generate_embedding(chunk)
        embedding_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
 
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            (EMBEDDING_ID, SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID, CHUNK_INDEX,
             CHUNK_TEXT, EMBEDDING, METADATA)
            VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?), ?)
        ''', (
            embedding_id, source_type, source_id, idx,
            chunk, str(embedding),
            json.dumps(metadata) if metadata else None
        ))
 
    conn.commit()
    print(f'Stored {len(chunks)} chunks for {source_type}/{source_id}')

Embedding generation has a cost per token through AI Core. For initial ingestion of large SAP document sets, run embedding generation in batches with rate limiting and store results immediately — do not regenerate embeddings on every pipeline run. Re-embed only when source documents change.

STEP 3: SIMILARITY SEARCH IN HANA SQL 

Querying the Vector Store with COSINE_SIMILARITY

How HANA Vector Search Works

HANA Cloud's vector engine computes similarity between a query embedding and all stored embeddings directly in SQL using built-in functions. COSINE_SIMILARITY measures the angle between vectors — returning values between -1 and 1, where 1 is identical. For SAP document retrieval, cosine similarity consistently outperforms L2 distance because it is scale-invariant — document length differences do not artificially inflate distance scores.

Similarity Search Function

# Step 3: Similarity search — retrieve top-k most relevant chunks
def similarity_search(query: str, source_type: str = None,
                      top_k: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Embed query and retrieve most similar chunks from HANA vector store.
    Optionally filter by source_type for targeted retrieval.
    """
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(query)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Build query with optional source type filter
    source_filter = "AND SOURCE_TYPE = ?" if source_type else ""
    params = [str(query_embedding), top_k]
    if source_type:
        params.insert(1, source_type)
 
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT TOP ?
            SOURCE_TYPE,
            SOURCE_ID,
            CHUNK_INDEX,
            CHUNK_TEXT,
            METADATA,
            COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY_SCORE
        FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
        WHERE 1=1 {source_filter}
        ORDER BY SIMILARITY_SCORE DESC
    ''', params)
 
    results = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        results.append({
            'source_type': row[0],
            'source_id':   row[1],
            'chunk_index': row[2],
            'chunk_text':  row[3],
            'metadata':    json.loads(row[4]) if row[4] else {},
            'score':       float(row[5])
        })
    return results

Filter by SOURCE_TYPE before running vector similarity whenever you know which data domain is relevant to the query. Narrowing the search space improves both retrieval precision and query performance — especially as your vector store grows across multiple SAP data domains.

STEP 4: THE COMPLETE RAG PIPELINE 

Assembling the End-to-End RAG Pipeline

With embedding generation and similarity search in place, the RAG pipeline itself is straightforward: embed the query, retrieve relevant chunks, assemble them as context, and pass context plus query to the foundation model. The model's response is grounded in your retrieved SAP data.

# Step 4: Complete RAG pipeline function
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
def rag_query(question: str, source_type: str = None,
              top_k: int = 5, system_context: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant SAP context,
    then generate grounded response using foundation model.
    """
    # Step 1: Retrieve relevant context from HANA vector store
    retrieved = similarity_search(question, source_type, top_k)
 
    # Step 2: Assemble context from retrieved chunks
    context_blocks = []
    for r in retrieved:
        context_blocks.append(
            f"[Source: {r['source_type']} / {r['source_id']} ",
            f"| Score: {r['score']:.3f}]\n{r['chunk_text']}"
        )
    context = '\n\n---\n\n'.join(context_blocks)
 
    # Step 3: Build grounded prompt with retrieved context
    default_system = '''You are an SAP expert assistant.
    Answer questions using ONLY the context provided below.
    If the context does not contain enough information to answer,
    say so explicitly — do not use general knowledge.
    Always reference the source when citing specific information.'''
 
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=system_context or default_system),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
CONTEXT FROM SAP DATA:\n{context}
\n\nQUESTION: {question}''')
    ]
 
    # Step 4: Generate grounded response
    return llm(messages).content

The instruction 'Answer using ONLY the context provided — do not use general knowledge' is critical for SAP RAG pipelines. Without it, the model supplements retrieved context with training data, which can introduce information about standard SAP that contradicts your specific configuration or customizations.

FOUR SAP RAG USE CASES 

Use Case Examples

  ▶  Use Case 1: SAP Knowledge Base Search 

Grounded Q&A on SAP Documentation

Ingest your SAP knowledge base articles, configuration guides, and internal runbooks into the vector store. The RAG pipeline then answers practitioner questions grounded in your actual documented procedures — not generic SAP guidance from the model's training data.

# Ingest SAP knowledge base articles
kb_articles = [
    {
        'id': 'KB-2024-001',
        'title': 'HANA Delta Load: Handling Large Volume Tables',
        'content': '''When delta loading tables exceeding 500M records...
        Use hash-based partitioning with MOD(ABS(TO_BIGINT(KEY_FIELD)), N)
        to distribute load across parallel workers. Monitor memory via
        M_HEAP_MEMORY view. Control table must track per-partition watermarks.'''
    },
]
 
for article in kb_articles:
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='KB',
        source_id=article['id'],
        text=f"{article['title']}\n\n{article['content']}",
        metadata={'title': article['title']}
    )
 
# Query the knowledge base
answer = rag_query(
    question='How should I handle integer overflow in HANA delta loads?',
    source_type='KB',
    top_k=3
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 2: HANA Data Dictionary Search 

Natural Language Queries on Table and Field Metadata

Embed HANA data dictionary metadata — table descriptions, field definitions, domain values, foreign key relationships — so practitioners can find relevant tables and fields using natural language instead of needing to know exact technical names upfront.

# Ingest HANA data dictionary metadata from HANA system views
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS,
           STRING_AGG(C.COLUMN_NAME || ': ' || IFNULL(C.COMMENTS,''),  ', ')
           AS FIELD_LIST
    FROM SYS.TABLES T
    JOIN SYS.TABLE_COLUMNS C ON T.TABLE_NAME = C.TABLE_NAME
    WHERE T.SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND T.TABLE_NAME LIKE 'Z%'
    -- Scope to your custom Z-tables first
    GROUP BY T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS
    LIMIT 500
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    table_name, description, fields = row
    content = f'Table: {table_name}\nDescription: {description}\nFields: {fields}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='DDIC',
        source_id=table_name,
        text=content,
        metadata={'table_name': table_name}
    )
 
# Find relevant tables using natural language
answer = rag_query(
    question='Which tables store customer payment terms and credit limits?',
    source_type='DDIC',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 3: Transport and Change Log Retrieval 

Semantic Search Across SAP Change History

Transport request descriptions and change logs are high-value institutional knowledge that is almost always searched by keyword — missing semantic connections between related changes. Embedding transport history enables semantic retrieval: find transports related to a concept or problem even when the exact terms don't match.

# Ingest transport descriptions from E070 / E07T
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER,
           STRING_AGG(O.OBJECT || '/' || O.OBJ_NAME, ', ') AS OBJECTS
    FROM E070 T
    JOIN E071 O ON T.TRKORR = O.TRKORR
    WHERE T.AS4DATE >= '20250101'
    AND T.TRSTATUS = 'R'
    -- Released transports only
    GROUP BY T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    tr_id, description, date, user, objects = row
    content = f'''Transport: {tr_id}
Description: {description}
Changed by: {user} on {date}
Objects: {objects}'''
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='TRANSPORT',
        source_id=tr_id,
        text=content,
        metadata={'tr_id': tr_id, 'date': str(date), 'user': user}
    )
 
# Semantic search across transport history
answer = rag_query(
    question='What changes were made to the FI posting handler in 2025?',
    source_type='TRANSPORT',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 4: Master Data Context Retrieval for AI Classification 

Retrieval-Augmented Master Data Classification

This use case combines the master data classification pipeline from Part 3 with RAG — retrieving similar previously-classified records as few-shot examples at query time. The model classifies new records guided by real examples from your own SAP master data, not generic training examples. This produces significantly higher accuracy on company-specific classification schemes.

# Ingest already-classified master data records as reference examples
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT MATNR, MAKTX, MATKL, MTART, ZZCATEGORY
    FROM MARA JOIN MAKT ON MARA.MATNR = MAKT.MATNR
    WHERE MAKT.SPRAS = 'E'
    AND ZZCATEGORY IS NOT NULL
    -- Only records with confirmed classifications
    LIMIT 1000
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    matnr, desc, matkl, mtart, category = row
    content = f'Material: {matnr} | {desc} | Type: {mtart} | Category: {category}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='MASTER_DATA',
        source_id=matnr,
        text=content,
        metadata={'matnr': matnr, 'category': category, 'mtart': mtart}
    )
 
# Classify new material using retrieved similar records as examples
new_material = 'Hydraulic seal kit 250bar SAP-HYD-250'
 
answer = rag_query(
    question=f'Classify this new material: {new_material}',
    source_type='MASTER_DATA',
    top_k=5,
    system_context='''You are an SAP master data classifier.
    Use the similar records provided as classification examples.
    Return JSON only: {"category": "...", "confidence": "High/Medium/Low",
    "based_on": [list of similar MATNR values used as reference]}'''
)
print(answer)

Retrieval-augmented classification using your own historical master data consistently outperforms zero-shot classification because the model is guided by your specific category labels and conventions — not generic industry taxonomies. The more classified records you ingest, the more accurate the retrieval and the better the classification output.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going to Production

 

Consideration

Guidance

Embedding consistency

Never mix embedding models across records in the same table. A similarity search comparing vectors from different models produces meaningless scores. Pick one model and stick to it — if you change models, re-embed everything.

Chunk overlap

50-token overlap between chunks prevents relevant context from being split across chunk boundaries. Adjust overlap up for dense technical content like ABAP code or configuration tables.

Score thresholds

Not all retrieved chunks are relevant. Filter results below a COSINE_SIMILARITY score of 0.75 before passing to the model. Low-score chunks add noise and reduce answer quality.

Re-embedding cadence

SAP data changes. Transport descriptions, knowledge base articles, and master data classifications go stale. Build a delta re-embedding job that runs on the same schedule as your delta data loads.

Source type filtering

Always filter by SOURCE_TYPE when the query domain is known. Mixing transport history chunks with knowledge base chunks in a single retrieval pass produces lower precision than targeted retrieval from the right domain.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI with OData APIs, S/4HANA integration, and the RAG pipeline from this article embedded in a CAP service
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The vector store you build in this article carries forward into Parts 5 and 6. If you're following the series end-to-end, the RAG pipeline here is the foundation that makes the CAP application and Joule extension in the next two parts genuinely useful rather than demo-grade. Drop a comment if you hit issues with REAL_VECTOR column dimensions or similarity score thresholds — these are the two most common friction points in production deployments.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Building AI-Powered CAP Applications on BTP (Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 5 OF 6  |  SAP BTP  |  CAP  |  AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: Building AI-Powered CAP Applications on BTP

End-to-end architecture for AI-powered SAP extension applications using CAP — project setup, AI Core service binding, HANA Cloud persistence, Generative AI Hub integration in service handlers, SAP Integration Suite connectivity, and Cloud Foundry deployment.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 5 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is the standard framework for building SAP extension applications on BTP. When combined with AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, CAP becomes the production layer that exposes your AI pipelines as governed OData and REST APIs — consumable by S/4HANA, Fiori, Integration Suite, and any external system. This article covers the complete path: CAP project setup, AI Core service binding, HANA Cloud persistence, calling the Generative AI Hub from CAP service handlers, Integration Suite connectivity, and Cloud Foundry deployment — built around two production-ready use cases.

Series Context

This is Part 5 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–4 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, and RAG on HANA Cloud vector store. This part moves from standalone Python scripts to production SAP applications — packaging the AI capabilities from previous parts into CAP services that integrate natively with the SAP ecosystem.

The RAG pipeline from Part 4 and the prompt pipelines from Part 3 are referenced throughout this article. If you are following the series end-to-end, those implementations carry forward directly into the CAP service handlers built here.

WHY CAP FOR SAP AI APPLICATIONS 

Why CAP Is the Right Production Layer for SAP AI

SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is SAP's opinionated framework for building cloud-native extension applications on BTP. It handles OData protocol compliance, HANA Cloud schema management, BTP service binding, multi-tenancy, and authorization out of the box — letting you focus on business logic rather than infrastructure plumbing.

For SAP AI applications specifically, CAP provides three capabilities that matter in production:

  • Native BTP service binding — AI Core, HANA Cloud, and Destination Service credentials are injected automatically at runtime via VCAP_SERVICES, eliminating manual credential management
  • OData and REST API exposure — your AI pipelines become consumable APIs that S/4HANA, SAP Fiori, and SAP Integration Suite can call directly without custom integration code
  • HANA Cloud persistence — the same HANA Cloud instance that holds your vector store from Part 4 also persists CAP entities, keeping your entire data layer consolidated

The result is that the prompt pipelines and RAG architecture from earlier in this series stop being Python scripts and become governed, auditable, production-grade SAP services — with the access controls, monitoring, and deployment lifecycle that enterprise SAP landscapes require.

CAP is not a replacement for AI Core or the Generative AI Hub — it is the application layer that sits in front of them. AI Core handles model access, authentication, and governance. CAP handles API exposure, service binding, and SAP ecosystem integration. Both are required for production SAP AI applications.

ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

End-to-End Architecture

The architecture for an AI-powered CAP application on BTP connects five layers:

S/4HANA / Fiori / Int. Suite → CAP OData/REST API → AI Core + Gen AI Hub →	HANA Cloud + Vector Store → Foundation Models

 

Layer

Role

S/4HANA / Fiori / Integration Suite

Consumers of the CAP API — send queries, receive AI-grounded responses

CAP Application (BTP CF)

OData and REST service layer — handles requests, calls AI Core, returns responses

SAP AI Core + Generative AI Hub

Foundation model access — GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Mistral via governed API

HANA Cloud

Dual purpose — CAP entity persistence AND vector store from Part 4

SAP Integration Suite

Orchestrates AI-augmented process flows across SAP and non-SAP systems

STEP 1: CAP PROJECT SETUP 

Setting Up the CAP Project

Prerequisites

Before initializing the CAP project, confirm the following are in place on your development machine:

  • Node.js 20 or higher — CAP requires a supported LTS version
  • @sap/cds-dk installed globally — the CAP development kit
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — for BTP deployment
  • SAP BTP subaccount with AI Core (Extended plan), HANA Cloud, and BTP CF runtime entitlements
  • AI Core resource group configured and service key available (covered in Part 1)

Initialize the CAP Project

# Install CAP development kit globally if not already installed
npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
 
# Initialize new CAP project
cds init ai-sap-service
cd ai-sap-service
 
# Install dependencies
npm install
 
# Add HANA Cloud support
cds add hana
 
# Add Cloud Foundry deployment configuration
cds add cf-manifest
 
# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK
npm install @sap-ai-sdk/langchain @sap-ai-sdk/ai-api

The @sap-ai-sdk/langchain package is SAP's official JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for the Generative AI Hub — the Node.js equivalent of the generative-ai-hub-sdk used in Parts 2–4. It handles OAuth token management, deployment ID resolution, and model routing automatically within your CAP service handlers.

Project Structure

After initialization, your CAP project structure looks like this:

ai-sap-service/
├── db/
│   └── schema.cds          # HANA Cloud entity definitions
├── srv/
│   ├── ai-service.cds      # OData service definition
│   └── ai-service.js       # Service handler — AI logic lives here
├── app/                    # Optional Fiori UI
├── mta.yaml               # BTP deployment descriptor
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json            # CAP configuration

STEP 2: DATA MODEL AND SERVICE DEFINITION 

Defining the Data Model and OData Service

HANA Cloud Entity Schema (db/schema.cds)

Define the entities that CAP will deploy to HANA Cloud. These persist AI query history, knowledge base documents, and search results — enabling audit trails and caching for production workloads.

// db/schema.cds
namespace ai.sap.service;
 
entity AIQueryLog {
    key ID          : UUID;
    query           : String(2000);
    response        : LargeString;
    modelUsed       : String(100);
    sourceType      : String(50);   // 'MASTER_DATA' | 'KNOWLEDGE_BASE'
    confidenceScore : Decimal(4,3);
    createdAt       : Timestamp;
    createdBy       : String(100);
}
 
entity KnowledgeBaseDoc {
    key ID          : UUID;
    title           : String(500);
    content         : LargeString;
    category        : String(100);
    embeddingStatus : String(20);   // 'PENDING' | 'EMBEDDED' | 'FAILED'
    createdAt       : Timestamp;
}

OData Service Definition (srv/ai-service.cds)

Define the OData service that exposes your AI capabilities. CAP generates a fully compliant OData V4 API from this definition — no manual API implementation required.

// srv/ai-service.cds
using ai.sap.service from '../db/schema';
 
service AISapService @(path: '/ai-service') {
 
    // Expose query log as read-only OData entity
    @readonly
    entity QueryLog as projection on ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog;
 
    // Expose knowledge base docs with full CRUD
    entity KnowledgeDocs as projection on ai.sap.service.KnowledgeBaseDoc;
 
    // Custom action: AI-powered master data search
    action searchMasterData(query: String, maxResults: Integer)
        returns array of {
            materialNumber: String;
            description:   String;
            similarity:    Decimal;
            aiSummary:     String;
        };
 
    // Custom action: RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A
    action askKnowledgeBase(question: String, category: String)
        returns {
            answer:   String;
            sources:  array of { docId: String; title: String; score: Decimal; };
            modelUsed: String;
        };
}

CAP generates the full OData V4 metadata document, entity CRUD operations, and batch processing from the .cds definition automatically. The custom actions (searchMasterData, askKnowledgeBase) are where your AI logic runs — implemented in the service handler in the next step.

STEP 3: AI CORE SERVICE BINDING 

Binding AI Core and HANA Cloud to the CAP Application

BTP Service Instances

Create and bind the required BTP service instances to your CAP application. In BTP Cloud Foundry, service binding injects credentials into VCAP_SERVICES at runtime — the SAP AI SDK reads these automatically.

# Create AI Core service instance (Extended plan required for Gen AI Hub)
cf create-service aicore extended ai-core-instance
 
# Create HANA Cloud service instance
cf create-service hana-cloud hana ai-hana-instance
 
# Create Destination service instance (for Integration Suite connectivity)
cf create-service destination lite ai-destination-instance

MTA Deployment Descriptor (mta.yaml)

The mta.yaml file defines your CAP application's deployment configuration and service bindings for BTP. CAP generates a base version with cds add cf-manifest — extend it to include AI Core binding:

# mta.yaml — key sections for AI Core binding
modules:
  - name: ai-sap-service-srv
    type: nodejs
    path: gen/srv
    parameters:
      buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
      memory: 512M
    requires:
      - name: ai-core-instance      # AI Core binding
      - name: ai-hana-instance      # HANA Cloud binding
      - name: ai-destination-instance
 
resources:
  - name: ai-core-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: aicore
      service-plan: extended
  - name: ai-hana-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: hana-cloud
      service-plan: hana
  - name: ai-destination-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: destination
      service-plan: lite

The AI Core Extended plan is required for Generative AI Hub access. The Standard plan provides AI Core infrastructure for custom model training but does not include Generative AI Hub model access. Verify your BTP subaccount has the Extended plan entitlement before deploying.

STEP 4: AI LOGIC IN CAP SERVICE HANDLERS 

Implementing AI Logic in CAP Service Handlers

The service handler file (srv/ai-service.js) is where your AI logic runs. CAP routes incoming OData action calls to the handler functions registered here. The SAP AI SDK handles AI Core authentication automatically using the VCAP_SERVICES credentials injected at runtime.

  ▶  Use Case 1: AI-Powered Master Data Search 

searchMasterData Action Handler

This handler receives a natural language search query, converts it to a vector embedding using the SAP embedding model, retrieves similar master data records from the HANA vector store built in Part 4, then generates an AI summary for each result. The response is returned as a structured OData collection.

// srv/ai-service.js
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const { ChatOpenAI } = require('@sap-ai-sdk/langchain');
const { OpenAIEmbeddings } = require('@sap-ai-sdk/langchain');
 
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
 
    // Initialize AI models — SDK reads AI Core credentials from VCAP_SERVICES
    const llm = new ChatOpenAI({
        modelName: 'gpt-4o',
        resourceGroup: 'default'
    });
 
    const embeddings = new OpenAIEmbeddings({
        modelName: 'text-embedding-ada-002',
        // SAP embedding model via AI Core
        resourceGroup: 'default'
    });
 
    // Use Case 1: AI-powered master data search
    this.on('searchMasterData', async (req) => {
        const { query, maxResults = 5 } = req.data;
 
        // Step 1: Generate embedding for the search query
        const queryEmbedding = await embeddings.embedQuery(query);
 
        // Step 2: Similarity search in HANA vector store
        const db = await cds.connect.to('db');
        const results = await db.run(`
            SELECT TOP ${maxResults}
                SOURCE_ID as materialNumber,
                CHUNK_TEXT as description,
                COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(queryEmbedding)}'))
                AS similarity
            FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            WHERE SOURCE_TYPE = 'MASTER_DATA'
            ORDER BY similarity DESC
        `);
 
        // Step 3: Generate AI summary for each result
        const enriched = await Promise.all(results.map(async (row) => {
            const summary = await llm.invoke([{
                role: 'system',
                content: 'Summarize this SAP material record in one sentence. Be specific and technical.'
            }, {
                role: 'user',
                content: `Material: ${row.description}`
            }]);
 
            return {
                materialNumber: row.materialNumber,
                description:   row.description,
                similarity:    parseFloat(row.similarity.toFixed(4)),
                aiSummary:     summary.content
            };
        }));
 
        // Step 4: Log query to HANA for audit trail
        await db.run(INSERT.into('ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog').entries({
            ID:             cds.utils.uuid(),
            query,
            response:       JSON.stringify(enriched),
            modelUsed:      'gpt-4o',
            sourceType:     'MASTER_DATA',
            createdAt:      new Date().toISOString(),
            createdBy:      req.user?.id || 'system'
        }));
 
        return enriched;
    });

  ▶  Use Case 2: RAG-Backed Knowledge Base Q&A 

askKnowledgeBase Action Handler

This handler implements the full RAG pipeline from Part 4 inside a CAP service action — making it callable as an OData action from S/4HANA, Fiori, or Integration Suite. The category parameter enables scoped retrieval, limiting similarity search to a specific knowledge domain.

    // Use Case 2: RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A
    this.on('askKnowledgeBase', async (req) => {
        const { question, category } = req.data;
 
        // Step 1: Embed the question
        const questionEmbedding = await embeddings.embedQuery(question);
 
        // Step 2: Retrieve relevant chunks from HANA vector store
        const db = await cds.connect.to('db');
        const categoryFilter = category ? `AND JSON_VALUE(METADATA, '$.category') = '${category}'` : '';
 
        const chunks = await db.run(`
            SELECT TOP 5
                SOURCE_ID as docId,
                CHUNK_TEXT as content,
                METADATA,
                COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(questionEmbedding)}'))
                AS score
            FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            WHERE SOURCE_TYPE = 'KB'
            AND COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(questionEmbedding)}')) > 0.75
            ${categoryFilter}
            ORDER BY score DESC
        `);
 
        // Step 3: Assemble retrieved context
        const context = chunks.map(c =>
            `[Source: ${c.docId} | Score: ${c.score.toFixed(3)}]\n${c.content}`
        ).join('\n\n---\n\n');
 
        // Step 4: Generate grounded answer — ONLY from retrieved context
        const answer = await llm.invoke([{
            role: 'system',
            content: `You are an SAP expert assistant.
Answer questions using ONLY the context provided.
If the context does not contain enough information, say so explicitly.
Do not use general SAP knowledge — only the provided context.
Always reference the source document when citing specific information.`
        }, {
            role: 'user',
            content: `CONTEXT:\n${context}\n\nQUESTION: ${question}`
        }]);
 
        // Step 5: Build response with sources
        const sources = await Promise.all(chunks.map(async (c) => {
            const doc = await SELECT.one.from('ai.sap.service.KnowledgeBaseDoc')
                .where({ ID: c.docId });
            return {
                docId: c.docId,
                title: doc?.title || c.docId,
                score: parseFloat(c.score.toFixed(4))
            };
        }));
 
        // Step 6: Log to HANA for audit
        await db.run(INSERT.into('ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog').entries({
            ID:             cds.utils.uuid(),
            query:          question,
            response:       answer.content,
            modelUsed:      'gpt-4o',
            sourceType:     'KNOWLEDGE_BASE',
            createdAt:      new Date().toISOString(),
            createdBy:      req.user?.id || 'system'
        }));
 
        return {
            answer:    answer.content,
            sources,
            modelUsed: 'gpt-4o'
        };
    });
 
});

Every AI query is logged to HANA via the AIQueryLog entity. This audit trail is not optional in enterprise SAP deployments — it provides the traceability that compliance, security, and governance teams require. The log captures the query, response, model used, source type, timestamp, and user — everything needed for a complete audit record.

STEP 5: SAP INTEGRATION SUITE CONNECTIVITY 

Connecting via SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite acts as the orchestration layer between your CAP AI service and SAP backend systems. Rather than S/4HANA calling your CAP service directly, Integration Suite manages the flow — applying transformation, error handling, retry logic, and monitoring across the end-to-end process.

Destination Configuration

Create a BTP Destination pointing to your deployed CAP service. Integration Suite uses this destination to route calls to your AI endpoints:

# BTP Destination configuration for CAP AI service
Name:             AI_SAP_SERVICE
Type:             HTTP
URL:              https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
Authentication:   OAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID:        <CAP application client ID>
Token Service URL: https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Integration Suite iFlow Pattern

A typical AI-augmented process flow in Integration Suite follows this pattern — S/4HANA triggers a business event, Integration Suite calls your CAP AI service, the AI response enriches or routes the downstream process:

S/4HANA event →	iFlow trigger →	CAP AI action call → AI response → Process routing

Example use case: A new material is created in S/4HANA. Integration Suite captures the MaterialCreated event, calls the CAP searchMasterData action to check for similar existing materials, and routes the process based on the AI similarity score — flagging potential duplicates for review before the material is activated.

When calling CAP OData actions from Integration Suite iFlows, use the HTTP Receiver adapter with OAuth2 authentication pointing to the BTP Destination created above. Do not embed AI Core credentials directly in iFlow configurations — all AI credential management should remain within the CAP service layer and its BTP service bindings.

STEP 6: CLOUD FOUNDRY DEPLOYMENT 

Deploying to BTP Cloud Foundry

Build and Deploy

# Build the CAP project for production
cds build --production
 
# Build the MTA archive
mbt build
 
# Deploy to BTP Cloud Foundry
cf login -a https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
cf deploy mta_archives/ai-sap-service_1.0.0.mtar

Verify Deployment

# Check application status
cf apps
 
# Check service bindings are correctly attached
cf services
 
# Tail logs to confirm AI Core connection on startup
cf logs ai-sap-service-srv --recent

Test the Deployed OData Actions

Once deployed, test your AI actions via the OData endpoint. The CAP service exposes a metadata document at /ai-service/$metadata — verify it loads correctly before testing the actions:

# Test master data search action
curl -X POST 'https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/ai-service/searchMasterData' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"query": "hydraulic pump assembly", "maxResults": 3}'
 
# Test knowledge base Q&A action
curl -X POST 'https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/ai-service/askKnowledgeBase' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"question": "How should I handle delta load failures?", "category": "HANA"}'

A successful deployment produces two working OData actions callable from any SAP system or external client — AI-powered master data search and RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A — with full audit logging to HANA Cloud, governed AI Core authentication, and Integration Suite connectivity for S/4HANA process orchestration.

PRODUCTION DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going Live

 

Consideration

Guidance

AI Core credentials

Never hardcode credentials in CAP service handlers. Always use VCAP_SERVICES binding via mta.yaml. Hardcoded credentials are a security violation and will fail rotation.

HANA vector store alignment

The HANA Cloud instance bound to your CAP application must be the same instance containing the AI_VECTOR_STORE schema from Part 4. Mismatched instances is the most common deployment error.

Similarity score threshold

Keep the 0.75 COSINE_SIMILARITY threshold from Part 4 in your CAP handler. Removing it returns low-relevance chunks that degrade answer quality and increase token consumption.

Audit log privacy

The AIQueryLog entity captures user queries. Review your data privacy policy before deploying — in some organizations, logging user query content requires explicit data handling disclosure.

Integration Suite authentication

iFlows calling your CAP service must use OAuth2 via BTP Destination — not basic auth. Basic auth against CAP OData endpoints is deprecated in BTP Cloud Foundry environments.

CAP multitenancy

If your CAP application serves multiple SAP tenants, enable CAP's built-in multitenancy (cds add multitenancy) before deploying. Single-tenant deployments cannot be retrofitted for multitenancy without significant rework.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core pipelines, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The CAP service built in this article is the foundation for the Joule extension in Part 6. The OData actions defined here — searchMasterData and askKnowledgeBase — are called directly from the Joule skill implementation in the final part of this series. Keep your deployed CAP service running as you move to Part 6.

entry.title: A Use Case for HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph: AI‑Driven Tender Analysis
entry.content:
Introduction

Many companies face the same challenge: they need to match unstructured text, such as tenders, applications, or support tickets, with their internal data to make informed decisions. In our project, we tackled exactly this problem and worked on an application that connects text understanding, semantic search and structured data. Our primary objective was to acquire knowledge about the tools SAP offers for developing applications of this nature. We undertook this project as part of SAP’s Early Adopter Care program for the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine.

Our concrete use case was an internal tender analysis scenario within our company, focusing on public-sector calls for tenders. Deciding about potential participation requires answering several questions: Does the topic fit our company's portfolio, which skills and experience are required and do the required skills exist in our organization, are the necessary resources available? Instead of manually searching for this information through documents and databases, we wanted to automate this process. The goal was to build a system that can read and interpret the tender text, understand the company's internal skill profiles, and combine both to produce a meaningful recommendation.

To achieve this, we combined three capabilities:

  • semantic structuring of employee data using a knowledge graph
  • semantic representation of competency descriptions and CVs using vector embeddings
  • LLM-powered analysis to interpret and connect both worlds

This architecture allowed us to bring together unstructured text and structured data enabling precise matching and explainable results, thus building a foundation that can be reused for many other AI scenarios.

We implemented the application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), a cloud-native framework that seamlessly integrates with SAP HANA Cloud, SAP AI Core and the Generative AI Hub (GenAI Hub). To give you a clear picture of how these components work together, the solution diagram below illustrates the overall flow, from data ingestion to semantic processing to the final application.

Solution Diagram

Provisioning the Resources

To implement this architecture, we provisioned a set of cloud resources in SAP BTP , each of which contribute a specific capability to the overall workflow.

At the center of the setup is SAP HANA Cloud, which provides both the Knowledge Graph Engine (KGE) and the Vector Engine (VE), and in which the CAP application data is persisted. The KGE is not enabled by default; it must be activated when the HANA instance is created and comes with some technical requirements:

  • Triple Store activation: The KGE relies on the HANA Triple Store, which must be enabled as part of the instance configuration. Without this, RDF triples cannot be stored or queried.
  • Minimum memory requirement: The KGE requires a HANA instance with at least 48 GB memory. This is necessary because storing the graph, executing SPARQL and SQL statements, and inference processes such as subclass inference, domain/range inference, and transitive property expansion operate in-memory. Smaller instances cannot activate Triple Store at all.

For the AI workflows, we provisioned AI Core which includes the GenAI Hub. AI Core runs the embedding and LLM processing jobs, while the GenAI Hub provides access to the models used for these tasks. This allows the application to generate semantic representations of text documents (tenders and CVs in our use case) and to retrieve information of documents via prompts.

The application layer itself is deployed as a CAP service running in a Cloud Foundry environment. CAP binds to the HANA Cloud, AI Core, and GenAI Hub service instances through standard service bindings, i.e. by receiving all required credentials and endpoints through environment variables.

This provisioning model keeps the architecture modular: each capability (knowledge graph, vector search, AI processing, and application logic) is provided as a separate cloud resource. It also makes the system easy to extend.

Knowledge Graphs and Embeddings: The Semantic Foundation of Our Architecture

Before diving into the SAP-specific components, it is useful to look at the two core concepts that shape our solution: knowledge graphs and embeddings. Both address the same challenge from different angles: turning human-readable meaning into structured, machine-interpretable representation.

Knowledge Graphs: Representing Relations between Data Entities

A knowledge graph stores information not as tables, but as entities and relationships. Instead of using foreign keys and joins, it forms an explicit network of connected facts. This makes it easier to express complex relationships, integrate data, and run queries that follow semantic structures rather than rigid schemas.

At the heart of every knowledge graph is an ontology: a structured definition of entities, properties, and relationships that the graph represents. An ontology defines:

  • classes (e.g., Person, Skill, Project)
  • properties that connect them (e.g., hasSkill, requiresSkill)
  • hierarchies and subclasses (e.g., abstract classes or hierarchical organization units)
  • constraints and expected value types (e.g., each identifiable entity has exactly one ID of type int)

The ontology gives the graph its semantic structure. It tells the system what kinds of things exist in the domain and how they relate to each other. Without an ontology, a knowledge graph would be just a collection of disconnected triples.

Knowledge graphs are typically stored as RDF (Resource Description Framework) triples, with the following general structure:

Subject – Predicate – Object
  • Subject: the thing we are talking about
  • Predicate: the property or relationship
  • Object: the value or another entity

For example:

Person123 – hasSkill – Python

This triple structure is simple but flexible and extensible. It is especially suitable for representing domains where the data model evolves over time.

To query RDF data, we use SPARQL, a graph-oriented query language. Instead of specifying joins explicitly – as is required in SQL – SPARQL expresses patterns that should appear in the data. This approach is particularly powerful for dynamic or AI-generated queries, where the structure of the query may not be known in advance. While SQL’s traditional query optimization excels when working with predefined schemas and foreign keys, it becomes less flexible when relationships or entity types vary. SPARQL, by contrast, is built to operate on semantic relationships and can naturally accommodate evolving graph structures.

The conceptual difference becomes clear when comparing a typical SQL query—which depends on rigid tables and join conditions—with an equivalent SPARQL query, where relationships are expressed in a way that is closer to how users understand the domain. In SPARQL, entities and relationships are simply assumed to exist in the graph without requiring predefined foreign-key constraints.

SQL-Query example

SELECT
    u.UserID,
    u.JobPosition,
    u.OrgUnitID,
    s.SkillName,
    us.SkillLevel,
    us.SkillLevelName,
FROM Users u
JOIN UserSkills us ON u.UserID = us.UserID
JOIN Skills s ON us.SkillID = s.SkillID
WHERE u.Active = 1

SPARQL-Query example

PREFIX : <http://example.org/my-project#>
PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#>

SELECT ?person ?position ?skill ?skillName ?level
WHERE {
    ?person rdf:type :Person ;
            :isActive true ;
            :hasJobPosition ?position ;
            :hasUserSkill ?userSkill .
    ?userSkill :connectsToSkill ?skill ;
               :hasSkillLevel ?level ;
               :hasSkillLevelName ?levelName .
    ?skill :hasSkillName ?skillName .   
}

 In short:

  • SQL is designed for handling stable, well-defined tabular schemas with fixed relationships.
  • SPARQL is designed for handling semantic relationships, flexible graph structures, and dynamically changing query shapes, making it well suited for AI-generated scenarios.

Embeddings: Encoding Text Semantics as Vectors

Embeddings take a different approach. Instead of modelling explicit relationships, they encode the semantic meaning of text as high-dimensional vectors. Similar texts end up close to each other in vector space while unrelated texts are far apart. This enables semantic search, similarity matching and clustering. Embeddings are essential when working with documents like tenders or CVs, where meaning is not explicitly structured but must be inferred.

How This Maps to the SAP World: KGE and VE

SAP HANA Cloud provides built-in components that implement these concepts directly inside the database:

  • The Knowledge Graph Engine (KGE) stores RDF triples and executes SPARQL queries. It gives us explicit, queryable relationships between entities such as people, skills, and projects.
  • The Vector Engine (VE) stores embeddings and provides functions for calculating the cosine similarity between embeddings, which simplifies similarity searches. It allows us to compare tender documents with CVs or skill profiles on a semantic level.

Together, KGE and VE combine explicit knowledge in the form of the knowledge graph structure with implicit meaning in the form of text embeddings. This dual representation enables our application to extract relevant information from tender documents and map it to our data model of employee competencies.

In the next section, we will look at how we designed the ontology and processed the data to populate both engines.

Building the Knowledge Graph

Creating the knowledge graph was a multi-step process that combined data engineering, domain modeling, and graph construction. While some parts of the pipeline can be automated, the core design work requires domain knowledge and deliberate modeling decisions. In our project we followed a three-stage approach.

Selecting and Structuring Relevant Data

The first step was to identify which data should become part of the knowledge graph and how these pieces of information relate to each other. This is a classic data engineering task: understanding the domain, exploring the available data sources, and deciding which entities and relationships are relevant for the scenario.

In our case, this meant analyzing the existing SQL-based data model (employees, skills, projects, roles, and assignments) and determining which parts of it contain meaningful information for matching tenders with internal capabilities. This step cannot be easily automated; it requires domain knowledge to decide which entities and relationships between them matter for the business question and which attributes are relevant for which entity type.

Creating An Ontology

Once the structure of the ontology has been decided on, it can be translated into classes, properties, hierarchies, and constraints that structure the graph.

Our ontology is defined using Turtle (Terse RDF Triple Language), a human-readable syntax for expressing RDF data. Turtle is a W3C standard that provides a compact and intuitive way to describe semantic relationships. It is based on the following key syntax elements:

Prefixes: Define namespace shortcuts for cleaner code

@prefix : <http://example.org/our-project#> .
@prefix owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .

Classes: Define entity types using a owl:Class

:Person a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Person" ;
    rdfs:comment "Individual employee or consultant" .

Properties: Define relationships (ObjectProperty) or attributes (DatatypeProperty)

:possessesSkillWith a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain :Person ;
    rdfs:range :UserSkill .

This links a person to their skill instances which include, e.g., proficiency levels and experience.

Triples: The fundamental RDF structure (Subject-Predicate-Object)

:Person rdfs:subClassOf :IdentifiableEntity .

This reads as: "Person is a subclass of IdentifiableEntity"

The semicolon notation allows defining multiple properties of the same entity without repeating the subject. The following statement is equivalent to four separate triples, but much more readable:

:Person a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Person" ;
    rdfs:comment "Individual employee" ;
    rdfs:subClassOf :IdentifiableEntity .

To organize related concepts, we introduced abstract classes. They reduce redundancy by collecting shared properties in one place instead of repeating them across many similar classes. This makes the ontology easier to maintain and ensures consistency because updates to shared properties only need to be made once.

In addition to structuring the model, the ontology also defines formal constraints that describe how data in the knowledge graph is expected to look. These constraints specify rules and cardinalities, such as requiring each person to have exactly one UserID or ensuring that each project includes a start date. While SAP HANA Cloud cannot automatically enforce these constraints without a reasoner, they still serve as valuable documentation and help implementing validation checks using Shapes Constraint Language tools.

Creating Instances

The knowledge graph contains instances that are generated from data stored in a relational database. To connect both worlds, we defined a mapping that links SQL tables and columns to ontology properties. For example, the ontology property hasUserID corresponds to the column UserID in the table Users in an SQL database. This declarative mapping makes it possible to extract relational data and convert it into semantic triples .

Simple one‑to‑one and one‑to‑many relationships can be handled directly. For many‑to‑many relationships, we added an extended mapping layer that can follow junction tables in the relational schema.

Because SAP HANA Cloud’s RDF Store does not include a reasoner, inverse properties cannot be inferred automatically. They must be created explicitly during triple generation. To support this, our mapping configuration allows us to define inverse property pairs (e.g., tagsProject ↔ projectTaggedWith), so the parser generates both directions of the relationship automatically.

The triple generation pipeline runs in several steps. First, it creates datatype property triples (literal attributes), automatically assigning the correct types based on the ontology. Then it generates object property triples (links between entities), using an in-memory index to quickly look up target URIs. For large datasets, the system uses chunked batch processing to load triples in bulk, which improves performance and reduces memory usage.

Each ontology class is written into its own RDF graph, allowing updates to specific entity types without reloading the entire knowledge base. Finally, the system serializes these graphs into Turtle (.ttl) files and uploads them to the SAP Knowledge Graph Engine (i.e., SAP HANA Cloud’s RDF Store).

Requirement Analysis and Evaluation

Extracting required skills and experience levels via Prompts

To extract information from tender documents, we developed an LLM‑based pipeline that first checks whether a tender fits the company’s domain. For tenders that match the business profile, the pipeline automatically generates a concise text summary and extracts the skills referenced in the document. For each identified skill, it captures the skill name, and its stated importance level in the tender — classified as must-have, should-have, or nice-to-have (when explicitly indicated in the text).

Skills and priorities

Skill Matching

This structured extraction turns free‑text requirements into machine‑readable skill profiles that can be directly queried within our knowledge graph. For these queries, the system needs to match the skills extracted by the LLM with the skill names stored in the employee database. These names rarely match exactly because of synonyms or different wording. We have implemented this matching by first looking up the top-k skills most similar in terms of word embedding using the Knowledge Graph Engine and subsequently filtering out entries that are unrelated to the skill mentioned in the tender via a prompt (for example, matching “Python” in the tender to entries like “Python 3” or “Python Programming” in the employee database).

Once the skill mapping is complete, the matched skills are inserted into a predefined SPARQL query. This query returns all employees who have these skills, including their proficiency levels, enabling automated candidate identification for tender requirements.

By combining embeddings with LLMs, the system can handle linguistic variations while still generating precise SPARQL queries.

Users matching

 

Requirement Evaluation

Once employees with the required skills are found through the SPARQL queries, the system calculates a skill score based on their proficiency levels. A skill receives a score of 1.0 if at least one employee is an expert, 0.8 for intermediate proficiency, 0.5 for beginner-level proficiency, and 0.0 if no one in the company has that skill. This gives an overall picture of how well the organization covers each required competency.

The skill scores are aggregated within the importance levels (must‑have, should‑have, nice‑to‑have) using a simple average. The result is a score for each importance level that shows how well the company’s employees' competencies match the tender requirements at different levels of importance.

Aggregated scores

Supplementary Analysis: Comparing Employee CVs and Tender Text

Beyond the structured skill matching outlined above, we added a second analysis pipeline which determines the suitability of candidates for a given tender document by evaluating the semantic similarity between employee CVs and tender text based on embeddings.

Employee CVs are split into meaningful chunks (up to 6,000 tokens each) so that related information about projects and skills stays together. Each chunk is then embedded using a suitable model deployed through the SAP AI Core and stored in SAP Vector Engine (i.e. SAP HANA Cloud’s vector database) for semantic search. Tender documents go through the same embedding process, turning unstructured text into dense vectors that capture meaning beyond simple keywords.

To identify suitable candidates, the system compares CV embeddings with tender embeddings using cosine similarity, ranking employees according to how closely their experience aligns with the project requirements. By capturing semantic similarity rather than relying on exact keyword matches, this approach enables accurate matching even when different wording or terminology is used.

By combining precise skill matching through the knowledge graph with semantic search across CV embeddings, the system provides a comprehensive way to find candidates based both on explicitly listed competencies and experience descriptions.

Outlook

As a next step, we plan to enable fully automated tender analysis where LLMs generate SPARQL queries directly from the tender text, without relying on predefined templates. This means the LLM would translate project requirements into precise knowledge graph queries on its own. For example, if a tender asks for “three senior Java developers with cloud architecture experience and at least five years in the financial sector,” the LLM would automatically create a SPARQL query that filters employees by skill levels, domain experience, and project history, returning all candidates who meet these criteria in one step.

entry.title: Integrating a Custom AI Model into SAP CAP Using AI Launchpad
entry.content:
As we all know AI is spreading across industries like wildfire and a lot of companies want to bring their own custom models into their SAP setups. In this blog I will walk you through a hands-on example of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core. And then making it a callable service in SAP AI Launchpad and connecting it directly to a CAP backend.

Rather than relying on pre-built generative AI services, we demonstrate how to bring your own model, package it in Docker, register it in AI Launchpad, and call it from your backend using a deployment ID. The goal is to cover the whole journey from serving the model to getting real-time results right in your UI.

Requirements

If you are not running everything locally, you'll need admin roles for SAP AI Launchpad and AI Core. For a local setup, there's a build step. For running the trained model in Docker a GPU is recommended but not strictly mandatory. (A CPU will work, just slower) Make sure your machine has enough CPU power, memory and disk space. If you want GPU acceleration, you’ll need a compatible NVIDIA GPU, the right drivers, and CUDA support.

Getting Data Set For Model Training

You can grab image datasets from Roboflow, Kaggle, Google Open Images, or other publicly available sources. But if you plan to use your model for more than just experimenting, you have to check the licenses and usage rights carefully. Or just make your own dataset by collecting and labeling images for your actual use case. That way you’ve got control over both quality and compliance.

Running the Application Locally

The application is first run locally to verify that it works as expected after pushing the model to Docker.

The Dockerfile used for local testing

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the CAP application, the requires section in package.json is updated as follows:

"cds": {
  "sql": {
  },
  "requires": {
    "[production]": {
    },
    "[hybrid]": {
    },
    "barcode-ai": {
      "kind": "rest",
      "credentials": {
        "url": "http://localhost:8080",
        "requestTimeout": 300000
      }
    }
  },
  "auth": "xsuaa",
  "html5-runtime": true,
  "portal": true
},

On the UI side, users will upload barcode images through a FileUploader component.

<u:FileUploader
                    id="imageUploader"
                    name="myFileUpload"
                    fileType="jpg,jpeg,png"
                    placeholder="{i18n>selectBarcodeImg}"
                    change="onImageSelected"
                    width="300px" />

                <Button
                    text="{i18n>barcodeImgTrigger}"
                    type="Emphasized"
                    press="onAnalyzeBarcode"
                    class="sapUiMediumMarginTop" />

The controller then adds the uploaded image to a FormData object and fires off a POST request.

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Test: Please Select An Image");
        return;
      }

      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const formData = new FormData();
        formData.append("image", this._selectedFile);

        const response = await fetch(
          "/catalog/RoleSearchService/analyze-barcode",
          {
            method: "POST",
            body: formData
          }
        );

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Test: Cant Call Ai Srv");
        }

        const result = await response.json();
        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)
        );
      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Test: Barcode Srv Failed");
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

For local testing the service call in service.js points to your locally running API. With this end-to-end local test you can check if the API can spot barcodes in uploaded images. And if so, pull out their locations, values, types and confidence scores.

The request-response structure of the local test service

 

AI Launchpad Connections

Once you've pushed your project that includes the Dockerfile, serve.py, requirements.txt, and serving_template.yaml to the GitHub, head over to SAP AI Launchpad.

Application settings in AI Launchpad for local testing

Under SAP AI Core Administration, go to Git Repositories and add your GitHub URL. Once the repository is synced, open All Applications and then create a new application. Pick your repository, set the path to (.), and revision to (main).

With the application synced, move to ML Operations and open Scenarios. You will see the serving_template.yaml is picked up automatically as an executable. The template defines the Docker image to run, the container port, resource requests, and the imagePullSecret pointing to our Docker Hub credentials, which are registered separately under Docker Registry Secrets.

 

 

Next, make a Configuration under the scenario, linking the executable to a resource group.

One important detail here is that the serving_template.yaml must define the metadata annotations, labels, and spec fields as pipe-delimited strings rather than nested YAML objects. This is a requirement of the SAP AI Core ServingTemplate schema that differs from standard Kubernetes manifests.

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: barcode-reader-serving
  namespace: <your-namespace>
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: "barcode-scenario"
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0.0"
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        autoscaling.knative.dev/metric: rps
        autoscaling.knative.dev/target: "100"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: "<your-dockerhub-username>/barcode-reader:latest"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
              protocol: TCP
          command: ["uvicorn", "serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "0.5"
              memory: "512Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1Gi"

 

Once your configuration is set trigger a Deployment. Use the infer.m resource plan, as it gives enough compute. After a few minutes the deployment will reach Running status with a unique Deployment ID.

Deployment Details in SAP AI Launchpad

 


Model binding in the updated package.json

Take that Deployment ID and put it into the cds.requires section of package.json under the barcode-ai destination. Now you can pass base64-encoded images in the request and get back barcode predictions from your CAP service layer.

 

 

 

 

 

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Please choose an image first!");
        return;
      }
      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const arrayBuffer = await this._selectedFile.arrayBuffer();
        const base64 = btoa(
          new Uint8Array(arrayBuffer)
            .reduce((data, byte) => data + String.fromCharCode(byte), '')
        );
        const response = await fetch("/catalog/SIAService/analyzeBarcodeImage", {
          method: "POST",
          headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
          body: JSON.stringify({ image: base64 })
        });
        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Error in Ai srv call");
        }
        const raw = await response.json();
        const result = typeof raw.value === 'string' ? JSON.parse(raw.value) : raw.value;

        if (!result.barcodeValue) {
          this.byId("resultText").setText("No barcode detected in the image");
          return;
        }

        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          `Barcode: ${result.barcodeValue} | Type: ${result.confidence || "-"}`
        );

        MessageToast.show("Barcode Scanned Successfully!");

      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Barcode Analysis Failed: " + err.message);
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

 

this.on('analyzeBarcodeImage', async (req) => {
      try {
        const image = req.data.image;
        if (!image) {
          req.error(400, 'Image is required');
          return;
        }

        const barcodeAI = await cds.connect.to('<Your_Destination>');
        const deploymentId = '<deploymentId>';
        const response = await barcodeAI.send({
          method: 'POST',
          path: `/v2/inference/deployments/${deploymentId}/v2/models/barcode-model/infer`,
          headers: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'AI-Resource-Group': 'default'
          },
          data: {
            image: image
          }
        });

        // response.predictions → [{data, type, rect}]
        const predictions = response?.predictions || [];

        if (predictions.length === 0) {
          return JSON.stringify({ barcodeValue: null, confidence: null, message: 'Barcode not found' });
        }

        return JSON.stringify({
          barcodeValue: predictions[0].data,
          confidence: predictions[0].type,
          allResults: predictions
        });

      } catch (err) {
        console.error('analyzeBarcodeImage error:', err);
        req.error(500, 'Barcode inference failed');
      }
    });

 

Once we have everything wired up and the CAP app is running, we head back to the frontend and run our test by uploading two images, one with a barcode and one without.

Barcode Detection In Action Test1

Barcode Detection In Action Test2

Future Improvements

The big takeaway integrating custom models with AI Launchpad is straightforward. And once you’ve plugged it in, you can use it across all kinds of projects with remarkable ease. That smooth integration makes a huge difference.

You can level this up in a bunch of ways. For example fine-tune a model like YOLOv8 for tricky conditions like dim lighting or angled shots or add QR code support with minimal changes or handle damaged barcodes by searching for similar visual embeddings in a database.

Summary

This blog walked through the full integration path of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core and consuming it from a CAP backend. Instead of relying on a managed generative AI service, the model was packaged in Docker, registered in SAP AI Launchpad via a serving template, and called from the CAP backend using a deployment ID over the AI Launchpad Destination.

The result is a working barcode detection feature that runs entirely on SAP infrastructure, with the model hosted and scaled by AI Core and the business logic kept cleanly within the CAP service layer. And as we covered, this is just the start. The architecture is flexible. Fine-tune for complex real world scenarios, tack on QR codes, or deal with scenarios like damaged barcodes using similarity search.

Hopefully, this gives consultants a clear roadmap for moving past generic AI tools and plugging their own models into real world solutions. You're not limited to barcodes either. This blueprint works for custom vision models, language models, whatever you need.

If you have any questions or thoughts, feel free to leave a comment below.

Ege Aksöyek

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Extending Joule with Custom Skills & AI Agents (Part 6 of 6 -Series Complete)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 6 OF 6  |  JOULE  |  SAP BUILD  |  AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: Extending Joule with Custom Skills and AI Agents

The final part of this series brings the AI pipelines from Parts 1–5 into Joule — building custom Joule skills backed by the CAP service from Part 5, assembling a multi-skill Joule agent for SAP knowledge Q&A and exception log triage, and covering prerequisites, BTP Destination configuration, Action project setup, Skill Builder, Agent Builder, and deployment.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 6 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Joule Studio (part of SAP Build) is where the AI pipelines from this series become conversational SAP capabilities accessible to any business user. A Joule Skill is a structured, rule-based capability that calls an API action. A Joule Agent goes further — it plans, reasons, and orchestrates multiple skills to handle complex multi-step queries. This article covers the complete path from BTP prerequisites through skill creation, agent assembly, and production deployment, connected directly to the CAP OData service built in Part 5.

Series Context and What This Part Covers

This is Part 6 — the final part — of the SAP Generative AI Hub series. Parts 1 through 5 built a complete AI stack: Generative AI Hub setup, Python SDK integration, prompt pipelines, HANA Cloud vector store with RAG, and a production CAP application on BTP Cloud Foundry.

This part surfaces all of that through Joule — SAP's AI copilot — so business users can interact with your AI capabilities in natural language without knowing anything about APIs, embeddings, or model calls. The CAP OData actions from Part 5 (searchMasterData and askKnowledgeBase) become Joule skills. Those skills are assembled into a Joule agent that handles multi-step SAP queries autonomously.

Joule Studio is a fast-evolving product. The Skill Builder reached General Availability in mid-2025. The Agent Builder became Generally Available in January 2026. Always verify current prerequisites and supported regions in the SAP Help Portal before beginning setup — regional availability and entitlement requirements have changed across releases.

ARCHITECTURE: HOW IT ALL CONNECTS 

End-to-End Architecture for This Series

Before building, it helps to see how Joule Studio connects to everything built in Parts 1–5:

User asks Joule → Joule Agent reasons →	Skill triggered → CAP OData action → AI Core + HANA → Grounded answer

Joule receives the user's natural language input and determines which skill or agent to trigger based on the description you provide during skill creation. The skill calls the CAP OData action deployed in Part 5. The CAP service calls AI Core and the HANA vector store. The grounded AI response travels back through CAP → Joule → user. The user sees a natural language answer. The entire AI stack is invisible to them.

 

Layer

Role

Joule Studio (SAP Build)

Conversational UI layer — skill and agent builder, deployment, testing

BTP Destination

Connection bridge from Joule Studio to the CAP service from Part 5

Action Project (SAP Build)

Defines which CAP OData endpoints Joule can call and how

Joule Skill

Structured capability — maps user intent to a specific Action call

Joule Agent

Autonomous orchestrator — plans and executes multiple skills for complex queries

CAP Service (Part 5)

Backend — AI Core calls, HANA vector retrieval, audit logging

STEP 1: PREREQUISITES AND BTP SETUP 

Prerequisites Before Opening Joule Studio

Joule Studio setup has more prerequisites than any other part of this series. Missing any of these will cause the deployment errors covered in the community Q&A section at the end of this article. Verify each item before proceeding.

Required BTP Entitlements

In your BTP Global Account, confirm the following entitlements are available and assigned to your subaccount:

Service

Plan

Note

SAP Build Process Automation

build-default (Application) plan

Required for Joule Studio access

Joule

foundation or standard (Application) plan

Required for Joule copilot runtime

SAP Cloud Identity Services

Identity Authentication + Provisioning

Must be activated and configured

SAP AI Core

Extended plan

Already in place from Part 1

HANA Cloud

hana plan

Already in place from Parts 4–5

Run the Joule Booster

The Joule Booster in BTP Cockpit is the recommended setup path. It configures the Joule formation, establishes trust between Joule and your integrated SAP systems, and sets up the required BTP Build Work Zone subscription. Without the Booster completing successfully, Joule Studio deployments will fail with Tenant Administration errors.

  1. Open your BTP Global Account → Boosters → search 'Setting Up Joule'
  2. Select the Joule (das-application) plan → choose your subaccount
  3. Select SAP Build Process Automation from the Capability Packages dropdown
  4. Review the Formation Name and select the correct Build Process Automation system instance
  5. Complete the Booster — confirm the success message before proceeding

If the Joule Booster fails partway through, do not retry immediately. Check BTP → System Landscape → Formations to see if a partial Joule formation was created. Delete it before re-running the Booster — orphaned formations are the most common cause of repeated Booster failures.

Activate Joule Studio Capability Package

After the Booster completes, activate the Joule Studio capability package within SAP Build Process Automation:

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Settings → Joule Studio
  2. Activate the Joule Studio capability package
  3. Confirm the Skill Builder and Agent Builder tiles appear in the SAP Build Lobby
STEP 2: BTP DESTINATION CONFIGURATION 

Configuring the BTP Destination for the CAP Service

Joule Studio connects to backend services exclusively through BTP Destinations. The CAP service deployed in Part 5 must be registered as a destination with specific additional properties that make it visible to SAP Build and Joule Studio.

Create the Destination in BTP Cockpit

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → your Joule subaccount → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

Name:                  AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
Type:                  HTTP
URL:                   https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
Authentication:        OAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID:             <your CAP app client ID from BTP>
Client Secret:         <your CAP app client secret>
Token Service URL:     https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Required Additional Properties

These additional properties are mandatory — without them the destination is invisible to Joule Studio and SAP Build:

sap.processautomation.enabled   =  true
HTML5.DynamicDestination        =  true
WebIDEEnabled                   =  true

The sap.processautomation.enabled = true property is the most commonly missed configuration step. Destinations without this property do not appear in the Joule Studio destination browser, and there is no error message — the destination is simply absent from the list. Always verify this property is set before troubleshooting connectivity issues.

Register Destination in SAP Build Control Tower

After creating the BTP Destination, register it in SAP Build so Joule Studio can access it:
