  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Control Tower → Destinations
  2. Click Open in BTP Cockpit — confirm your destination appears in the list
  3. Return to Control Tower → Environments → Create or select your deployment environment
  4. In the environment, add the destination AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
  5. Click Check Connection — confirm the green checkmark before proceeding
STEP 3: ACTION PROJECT — EXPOSING CAP ENDPOINTS TO JOULE 

Creating Action Projects for the CAP OData Actions

An Action Project in SAP Build defines which endpoints from your CAP service Joule can call, what inputs they accept, and what outputs they return. Actions are the bridge between destinations and Joule Skills. You create one Action Project per service, then select individual actions within each skill.

Create the Action Project

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Connectors → Actions → Create
  2. In Choose an API Source → select SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  3. Browse OData Destinations → select AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
  4. Enter a name (e.g. AI Knowledge Service Actions) and description → Create

Configure the searchMasterData Action

From the list of available endpoints from your CAP service, select the searchMasterData action:

  1. Select POST searchMasterData from the endpoint list → Add
  2. On the Input tab — confirm query (String) and maxResults (Integer) are visible
  3. On the Output tab — confirm materialNumber, description, similarity, and aiSummary fields
  4. Use the Test tab to validate — enter a sample query and verify the response before saving
  5. Save → Release → Publish to Library

Configure the askKnowledgeBase Action

Repeat the process for the askKnowledgeBase action:

  1. Select POST askKnowledgeBase → Add
  2. Input tab — confirm question (String) and category (String, optional)
  3. Output tab — confirm answer, sources array, and modelUsed fields
  4. Test with a sample question — verify a grounded answer returns from your HANA vector store
  5. Save → Release → Publish to Library

Both actions must be Published to Library before they are visible in the Joule Skill Builder. Released but unpublished actions do not appear in the Browse All Actions dialog. If your actions are missing from the skill builder, check their publication status in Connectors → Actions.

STEP 4: BUILDING JOULE SKILLS 

Creating Custom Joule Skills in Skill Builder

Joule Skills are structured, deterministic capabilities — when Joule identifies a user intent that matches a skill's description, it triggers that skill and calls the configured action. Skills are ideal for focused, single-purpose tasks where the response structure is predictable.

The skill description is the most important field in the entire skill definition. Joule uses it to decide when to trigger this skill versus other skills or agents. A vague description produces inconsistent triggering. A precise description produces reliable triggering.

  ▶  Skill 1: SAP Knowledge Base Q&A 

Creating the askKnowledgeBase Skill

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Create → Select Joule Skill tile → Next
  2. Name: AskSAPKnowledgeBase
  3. Description (critical — Joule uses this for intent matching):
Answers questions about SAP configuration, HANA procedures, migration runbooks, and internal SAP knowledge base documentation. Use when the user asks 'how do I', 'what is the process for', 'explain', or asks about SAP-specific procedures, error handling, or best practices.
  1. Review → Create → the Skill Builder canvas opens
  2. In the Trigger panel → disable 'Allow Joule to generate a response' (the action will provide the response)
  3. Configure Skill Inputs → Add Input: question (String, Required), category (String, Optional)
  4. Configure Skill Outputs → Add Output: answer (String), sources (Array), modelUsed (String)
  5. On the canvas, choose + → Call Action → Browse All Actions
  6. Select askKnowledgeBase from AI Knowledge Service Actions → Add
  7. Select Destination Variable → Create → name it AI_KNOWLEDGE_DEST → select AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
  8. Map Inputs: question → question, category → category
  9. Map Outputs: answer → answer, sources → sources, modelUsed → modelUsed
  10. Add a Send Message step → configure the response card to display the answer and source documents
  11. Save → the skill is ready for testing

  ▶  Skill 2: Exception Log Triage 

Creating the Exception Log Triage Skill

This skill triggers the exception log triage pipeline from Part 3, exposed as a CAP OData action. Add an additional action to your Action Project for the exception log endpoint if not already present, then create the skill following the same pattern:

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Create → Joule Skill
  2. Name: SAPExceptionLogTriage
  3. Description:
Analyzes SAP exception logs, error dumps, and system messages. Use when the user provides an error log, exception text, or asks to analyze, triage, or diagnose an SAP system error or runtime failure.
  1. Configure Inputs: logText (String, Required — the raw log content)
  2. Configure Outputs: severity (String), summary (String), actions (String)
  3. Add Call Action step → select triageExceptionLog from Action Project
  4. Map inputs and outputs → Add Send Message step with severity-coded response card
  5. Save

Joule Skill descriptions must be precise and distinct from each other. If two skills have overlapping descriptions, Joule will trigger unpredictably between them. Test with a range of natural language inputs in the debug interface before deploying to confirm each skill triggers reliably for its intended query type.

  STEP 5: BUILDING THE JOULE AGENT 

Assembling a Multi-Skill Joule Agent

A Joule Agent goes beyond individual skills. Where a skill handles one focused task deterministically, an agent plans and reasons — it understands a complex user query, decides which skills to invoke and in what order, handles the outputs, and synthesizes a final response. Agents are built in Joule Studio's Agent Builder, which became Generally Available in January 2026.

Principal propagation is enforced automatically — the user's identity in Joule passes through to every skill and CAP service call. Your agent runs within the user's security context, applying the same authorization checks as any other SAP system access.

Create the Agent

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Create → Select Joule Agent tile → Next
  2. Name: SAPAIAssistant
  3. Agent Instructions (natural language — define the agent's role and boundaries):
You are an SAP AI assistant with access to the organization's SAP knowledge base, master data, and system logs. Help users find SAP configuration guidance, search for relevant materials, and triage SAP exception logs. Always ground your answers in the retrieved context. If you cannot find relevant information, say so explicitly rather than generating a general answer. For exception logs, always provide severity, root cause, and recommended action.
  1. Select Model — choose from available foundation models in your AI Core instance (GPT-4o or Claude recommended for reasoning quality)
  2. Save the base agent configuration

Add Skills as Agent Tools

Skills are added to the agent as callable tools. The agent decides at runtime which tool to invoke based on the user's query and its instructions:

  1. In the Agent canvas → Tools section → Add Tool → Select Joule Skill
  2. Add AskSAPKnowledgeBase → the skill description appears as the tool description the agent uses for routing decisions
  3. Add SAPExceptionLogTriage → confirm the description is distinct enough from the knowledge base skill
  4. Optionally add searchMasterData as a third tool if master data search is a common use case

The agent uses each skill's description to decide which tool to call. The same description precision principle from skill creation applies here — if the skill descriptions are vague or overlapping, the agent's tool selection will be unreliable. Review all skill descriptions together before finalizing the agent.

Configure Human-in-the-Loop (Optional but Recommended)

Joule Studio agents support human-in-the-loop by default. For exception log triage — where the recommended action may affect production systems — configure the agent to request confirmation before presenting recommended actions:

  1. In Agent Settings → Human-in-the-Loop → Enable
  2. Configure confirmation prompts for high-severity triage outputs
  3. This ensures a human reviews critical recommendations before they are surfaced to end users
STEP 6: TESTING IN JOULE STUDIO 

Testing Skills and the Agent Before Deployment

Joule Studio provides a built-in testing and debug interface. Use it before deploying to production — it shows exactly which skill or tool the agent selected, what inputs were passed, what the action returned, and how the response was assembled.

Testing Individual Skills

  1. Open the skill → Choose Launch in the top right
  2. A standalone Joule test interface opens
  3. Enter test queries that should trigger this skill — verify the action is called and the response is correct
  4. Enter queries that should NOT trigger this skill — verify it does not activate
  5. Review the Design Console for any configuration errors

Testing the Agent

  1. Open the agent → Choose Test in the top right
  2. The agent debug interface opens — this shows the full execution log
  3. Test with these queries to validate routing across all skills:

 

'How do I handle integer overflow in a HANA delta load procedure?'

→ Expected: AskSAPKnowledgeBase triggered
'Analyze this SAP log: RABAX INCLUDE ZFI_HANDLER Line 847...'

→ Expected: SAPExceptionLogTriage triggered
'Find materials similar to hydraulic pump assembly'

→ Expected: searchMasterData triggered (if added as tool)
  1. Expand the execution log nodes — confirm tool selection, input mapping, action response, and final output at each step
  2. Adjust agent instructions or skill descriptions if routing is incorrect — re-test after each change

The execution log in the Agent test interface is the most valuable debugging tool in Joule Studio. When agent behavior is unexpected, the log shows exactly why — which tool was selected, what prompt the agent generated, and what the action returned. Always check the log before adjusting instructions or skill descriptions.

STEP 7: DEPLOYING TO PRODUCTION JOULE 

Deploying Skills and Agent to Production

Deploy Skills

  1. Open each skill → Choose Deploy
  2. Select the deployment environment configured in Control Tower
  3. Confirm the destination AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE is selected for the destination variable
  4. Deploy → wait for status to show Deployed

Deploy the Agent

  1. Open the agent → Choose Deploy
  2. Select the same deployment environment
  3. Confirm all skills show as available tools in the deployment preview
  4. Deploy → the agent status changes to Active

Access via Joule Formation

Once deployed, your skills and agent are available through the Joule Formation — the configuration that connects Joule to your SAP landscape. Users access the custom capabilities through the same Joule interface they use in S/4HANA, Fiori, or SAP Build Work Zone. No additional UI development is required.

To verify deployment in the Joule Formation: BTP Cockpit → System Landscape → Formations → your Joule Formation → confirm the agent appears in the active capabilities list.

Skills and agents deployed in a development or test environment are not automatically available in the production Joule Formation. Each environment requires its own deployment. Confirm which environment is connected to your production Joule instance before deploying and testing with end users.

COMMON DEPLOYMENT ISSUES 

Issues You Will Encounter and How to Resolve Them

 

Issue

Root Cause

Resolution

Error 6011 — Tenant Administration Worker

Joule Booster did not complete successfully or a partial formation exists

Delete orphaned formations in System Landscape → re-run the Joule Booster completely

Destination not visible in Joule Studio

sap.processautomation.enabled = true missing from destination additional properties

Add the property in BTP Cockpit → Destinations → edit destination → Additional Properties

Action not visible in Skill Builder

Action was Released but not Published to Library

Open Action Project → Versions → Publish the released version to Library

Agent triggers wrong skill

Skill descriptions are overlapping or too generic

Rewrite skill descriptions to be distinct and intent-specific — test with boundary queries

404 on action call from skill

Destination variable in skill points to wrong environment or destination

Open skill → check destination variable mapping → re-deploy to correct environment

Principal propagation failing

Identity Authentication trust not configured between Joule subaccount and CAP service subaccount

Verify IAS trust configuration in both subaccounts — both must use the same IAS tenant

Series Complete — What You've Built

Across six articles, this series built a complete SAP Generative AI Hub stack from first API call to production Joule agent:

  • Part 1: BTP entitlements, AI Core service instance, resource group, first API call
  • Part 2: Python SDK across BAS notebooks, local scripts, and BTP applications — five SAP data use cases
  • Part 3: Prompt pipelines — sequential chaining and chain-of-thought reasoning across four SAP workflows
  • Part 4: HANA Cloud vector store — REAL_VECTOR schema, SAP embedding model, RAG on SAP data
  • Part 5: CAP application on BTP Cloud Foundry — AI-powered OData APIs with HANA persistence and Integration Suite connectivity
  • Part 6: Joule Studio — custom skills and multi-skill agent backed by the entire AI stack

The architecture that connects all six parts is the same architecture that SAP's own AI Foundation runs on. The Generative AI Hub provides governed model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. CAP provides the application and API layer. Joule Studio provides the conversational interface. Each layer builds on the previous one — and each one is production-ready, auditable, and governed within the SAP BTP trust boundary.

If you followed this series end-to-end and have questions about any specific step — or if you've implemented a variation of this architecture in your own SAP landscape — share it in the comments. The patterns in this series are starting points. The community variations and extensions are where the most valuable knowledge lives.

entry.title: Managing SAP AI Core from the Terminal with saic-cli
entry.content:
Managing SAP AI Core from the Terminal with saic-cli

Working with SAP AI Core day-to-day surfaces a few recurring pain points with SAP AI Launchpad:

  • No way to create custom scenarios through the UI
  • No bulk operations for prompt templates — creating or cleaning up templates means doing it one by one
  • Repetitive UI navigation for routine tasks like listing configurations or checking deployment status
  • No scripting support — any automation requires building custom API calls from scratch

saic-cli is a small CLI tool that brings all of this to the terminal — no UI, no manual API calls, just commands.

Getting Started

1. Install

npm install -g sap-aicore-cli

2. Connect to Your AI Core Instance

The setup command wraps cf login and stores your credentials:

saic-cli setup --sso -a https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

This is the only configuration you need. From here, every command picks up your credentials automatically.

What Makes It Useful

Beyond the commands themselves, a few things make saic-cli practical for day-to-day work:

Full API coverage — deployments, executions, configurations, prompt templates, secrets, repositories, resource groups, dataset files, and more. If it's in the SAP AI Core REST API, there's a command for it.

Zero config after setup — one saic-cli setup command is all it takes. Every subsequent command picks up credentials automatically, no extra configuration needed.

Scriptable by default — every command supports --json output, making it easy to pipe results into other tools or use in shell scripts.

Safe to automate — --dry-run lets you preview any write operation before it runs, useful in CI/CD pipelines.

Manage Deployments

# List running deployments
saic-cli list-deployments --status RUNNING

# Create a deployment from a configuration
saic-cli create-deployment --config-id <config-id>

# Stop and clean up
saic-cli update-deployment <id> --target-status STOPPED
saic-cli delete-deployment <id> --force

Configure Foundation Models

Creating a configuration for a foundation model like GPT-4o is a single command:

saic-cli create-configuration \
  --name "gpt-4o" \
  --executable-id "azure-openai" \
  --scenario-id "foundation-models" \
  --params '[{"key":"modelName","value":"gpt-4o"}]'

Manage Prompt Templates in Bulk

One area where the Launchpad falls short is prompt template management. saic-cli provides a small workflow to go from zero to a deployed template quickly.

Start by generating a ready-to-edit template scaffold:

saic-cli generate-template

This creates a folder with everything you need:

my-template/
├── prompt.yaml         # Main config: name, version, scenario, variables, LLM settings
├── system-prompt.md    # System role instructions (edit to define AI behavior)
└── user-prompt.md      # User role template with variable placeholders e.g. {{?InputText}}

The prompt.yaml looks like this:

name: MY_TEMPLATE
version: 1.0.0
scenario: MY_SCENARIO

spec:
  template:
    - role: system
      contentFile: ./system-prompt.md
    - role: user
      contentFile: ./user-prompt.md
  variable_definition:
    InputText: ""
    OutputFormat: ""
  additional_fields:
    executableId: azure-openai
    llmName: gpt-4o

Edit the files to fit your use case, then do a dry run before deploying:

saic-cli create-prompt --dir ./my-template --dry-run

Once ready, deploy:

saic-cli create-prompt --dir ./my-template

You can also list and clean up templates by scenario:

# List templates in a scenario
saic-cli list-templates --scenario-id my-scenario

# Delete all templates in a scenario
saic-cli delete --scenario my-scenario

Scripting & Automation

Every command supports --json output, which makes it easy to pipe results into other tools:

# Get all running deployments as JSON
saic-cli list-deployments --status RUNNING --json

# Preview a command before running it
saic-cli create-deployment --config-id <id> --dry-run

The --dry-run flag is particularly useful in CI/CD pipelines — you can validate inputs without making any changes.

Other Commands

Beyond deployments and prompts, the CLI covers:

  • Executions & schedules — create, update, delete, and schedule AI executions
  • Secrets — manage Docker registry, object store, and generic secrets
  • Repositories & applications — onboard git repos and manage ArgoCD applications
  • Resource groups & services — full lifecycle management
  • Dataset files — upload, download, and delete files from object storage

Run saic-cli --help to see the full list.

Project Structure

saic-cli is a TypeScript CLI where each command is implemented as a self-contained plugin. Commands are lazy-loaded, so only what's needed gets initialized. Under the hood it uses the @sap-ai-sdk packages to talk to SAP AI Core.

sap-aicore-cli/
├── src/
│   ├── cli.ts                  # Entry point, command routing
│   ├── index.ts                # Library exports
│   ├── commands/               # Command plugins (one file per command)
│   │   ├── setup.ts            # Interactive setup wizard
│   │   ├── deployments.ts      # Deployment CRUD
│   │   ├── executions.ts       # Execution management
│   │   ├── create-prompt.ts    # Prompt template creation
│   │   ├── generate-template.ts# Template scaffold generator
│   │   └── ...
│   ├── api/                    # API wrappers (one file per resource)
│   │   ├── deployments.ts
│   │   ├── prompt-registry.ts
│   │   └── ...
│   └── utils/                  # Shared utilities

Closing Thoughts

saic-cli is still early-stage and covers common day-to-day use cases — but if you're working with SAP AI Core and find yourself reaching for the same Launchpad screens repeatedly, it might save you some clicks.

Feedback and contributions are welcome.

Links

  • npm: sap-aicore-cli
  • GitHub: mduw/sap-aicore-cli
  • SAP AI Core REST APIs

entry.title: SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TROUBLESHOOTING  |  SAP AI CORE  |  PART 1 OF 2

SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them

Every error between your first curl command and a working bearer token — what causes them, what they actually mean, and the exact fix for each one. Part 1 of 2.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP AI Core Troubleshooting Series  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Getting your first SAP AI Core bearer token working on Windows is harder than it should be. This article documents every authentication error between zero and a working token — PowerShell curl alias conflicts, quote character issues, port errors, missing OAuth path, empty responses, and RBAC denials — with the exact cause and fix for each. Part 2 covers deployment and configuration errors once authentication is working.

Why This Article Exists

The SAP AI Core documentation covers the happy path well. What it doesn't cover is the sequence of errors most practitioners hit on Windows before they ever get a working API call. These errors are not obscure — they come up consistently across different BTP setups, different teams, and different experience levels.

This article documents six authentication errors in the order they typically appear, based on real troubleshooting sessions. Each error has a clear cause and a specific fix. No generic advice — only what actually works.

Part 2 (Friday) covers what happens after authentication works: 404 on deployment calls, two-instance confusion, resource group provisioning failures, and orchestration vs foundation-models mismatches.

BEFORE YOU START 

What You Need

This guide assumes you have an SAP AI Core service instance on BTP with at least one service key created. From the service key JSON you need four values:

  • url — the OAuth authentication base URL (e.g. https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com)
  • clientid — your OAuth client ID
  • clientsecret — your OAuth client secret
  • serviceurls.AI_API_URL — your AI Core API base URL

The url field in your service key does NOT include /oauth/token. You must append it manually. This is the single most common cause of authentication failures and is covered in Error 5 below.

ERROR 1 OF 6  
  ERROR 1    PowerShell curl alias — -X not recognized    ParameterBindingException 
What You See
Invoke-WebRequest : A parameter cannot be found that matches parameter name 'X'.
At line:1 char:6
+ curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token'
What Causes It

PowerShell has a built-in alias called curl that actually runs Invoke-WebRequest — Microsoft's own HTTP cmdlet. Invoke-WebRequest uses completely different syntax and does not understand the -X flag that real curl uses. When you type curl in PowerShell you are not running curl.exe — you are running Invoke-WebRequest.

The Fix

Two options — use Command Prompt instead of PowerShell, or explicitly call curl.exe to bypass the alias:

# Option 1 — Open Command Prompt (cmd) instead of PowerShell
# All curl commands in this guide use double quotes — required in cmd
 
# Option 2 — Stay in PowerShell but force the real curl binary
curl.exe -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

All remaining examples in this guide use Command Prompt syntax with double quotes. If you prefer PowerShell, add .exe after curl and the commands will work identically.

ERROR 2 OF 6  
  ERROR 2    Curly quotes / bad characters    curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

When you copy a curl command from a web page, Teams message, email, or PDF, the quote characters are often converted to typographic curly quotes (“ ”) instead of straight ASCII quotes (" "). Curly quotes look identical on screen but are invisible corruption to curl — it cannot parse the URL correctly and reports a nonsensical port error.

This error is almost always a quote character issue. The URL itself is correct.

The Fix

Do not paste the URL from a rich text source. Type the URL manually, or paste into a plain text editor (Notepad, VS Code) first to strip formatting, then copy from there into the terminal.

Also ensure all quote marks in your command are straight ASCII double quotes (") — not curly (“”) or single (‘’). In Command Prompt, single quotes are not valid string delimiters at all.

# Wrong — curly quotes from copy-paste (looks fine, breaks silently)
curl -X POST “https://...authentication.../oauth/token”
 
# Correct — straight ASCII double quotes
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token"
ERROR 3 OF 6  
  ERROR 3    Single quotes in Command Prompt    curl: (3) URL rejected 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

Single quotes work as string delimiters in PowerShell and Linux/Mac terminals. They do not work in Windows Command Prompt. If you copy a curl command that uses single quotes (common in documentation written for Linux) and run it in cmd, Command Prompt interprets the single quote as part of the URL string — causing the same port parsing error as curly quotes.

The Fix

Replace all single quotes with double quotes when running curl in Command Prompt:

# Wrong — single quotes (Linux/Mac syntax, fails in Windows cmd)
curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
 
# Correct — double quotes (Windows cmd syntax)
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded"

In Command Prompt, line continuation uses ^ (caret) not \ (backslash). If you copy Linux curl commands with \ at the end of each line, remove them and replace with ^ or put the entire command on one line.

ERROR 4 OF 6  
  ERROR 4    Empty response — no output at all    Command exits with no output 
What You See

The curl command runs, the prompt returns, but nothing is printed. No error, no response, no JSON — just silence.

What Causes It

Three common causes in order of likelihood:

  • The -d parameter contains an & character that Command Prompt interprets as a command separator — splitting your command into two separate commands, one of which runs silently
  • A missing closing quote somewhere in the command causes cmd to wait for more input silently
  • The response is very large and the terminal is suppressing it — redirect to a file to capture it
The Fix

The & in client_credentials&client_id=... must stay inside double quotes. If it's outside quotes, Command Prompt treats it as a command separator:

# Wrong — & outside quotes splits the command
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" -d grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=abc
 
# Correct — entire -d value inside double quotes
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

If you suspect a large response is being suppressed, redirect output to a file:

curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ... > output.txt

Open output.txt — the full response will be there regardless of terminal display limitations.

ERROR 5 OF 6  
  ERROR 5    401 Unauthorized — missing /oauth/token path    {"error":"unauthorized"} 
What You See
{"error":"unauthorized","error_description":"An Authentication object was not found in the SecurityContext"}
What Causes It

The url field in your AI Core service key JSON is the base authentication domain — it does not include the /oauth/token path. Many practitioners use this URL directly and hit 401 because they're calling the base domain rather than the token endpoint.

Compare these two:

# What's in your service key url field (base domain — not the token endpoint)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com
 
# What you need to call (token endpoint — append /oauth/token manually)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
The Fix

Always append /oauth/token to the url value from your service key. The SDK handles this automatically — but when calling directly with curl you must add it yourself:

curl -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful token call returns a JSON object containing access_token — a long base64-encoded string. This is your bearer token. Copy everything between the quotes after "access_token": and use it in all subsequent AI Core API calls. Tokens expire after 3600 seconds (1 hour) — generate a fresh one for each session.

ERROR 6 OF 6  
  ERROR 6    RBAC access denied on resource group creation    {"error":{"code":"403"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"403","message":"RBAC: access denied"}}
What Causes It

RBAC access denied on a resource group PUT call means your service key does not have admin scope for this AI Core instance. Service keys are created with specific role assignments — a key created without aicore_admin scope can generate tokens and list resources but cannot create or modify resource groups.

This is not a token expiry issue and not a URL issue. The token is working — the credentials behind it simply don't have the permission required.

The Fix

First — check if a resource group already exists before trying to create one. Many AI Core instances provisioned by BTP have a default resource group already:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"

If a resource group already exists with status PROVISIONED — use it. There is no need to create a new one.

If you genuinely need to create a new resource group and are hitting RBAC denial, recreate the service key with admin permissions:

  1. BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → your AI Core instance → Service Keys
  2. Delete the existing service key
  3. Create a new service key — confirm aicore_admin role is included in the parameters
  4. Generate a new token using the new service key credentials

Check existing resource groups before attempting to create new ones. Most RBAC errors on resource group creation are unnecessary — the default resource group already exists and has status PROVISIONED. The GET call above takes 10 seconds and saves significant debugging time.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Authentication Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

ParameterBindingException — -X not recognized

PowerShell curl alias intercepting command

Use cmd or call curl.exe explicitly

Port number not decimal between 0 and 65535

Curly quotes from copy-paste corrupting URL

Type URL manually or paste through plain text editor first

Same port error with single quotes

Single quotes invalid in Windows cmd

Replace all single quotes with double quotes

No output — command exits silently

& in -d value treated as command separator by cmd

Keep entire -d value inside double quotes

401 — Authentication object not found

Calling base auth URL without /oauth/token suffix

Append /oauth/token to url value from service key

403 — RBAC access denied

Service key lacks aicore_admin scope

Check existing resource groups first — recreate key if creation is genuinely needed

THE WORKING COMMAND 

Complete Working Token Call for Windows Command Prompt

Once all six errors are resolved, this is the complete working token call. All values in capitals are replaced with your service key values:

curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful response looks like this — the access_token value is your bearer token:

{"access_token":"eyJhbGci...very long string...","token_type":"bearer","expires_in":3599}

Copy the full access_token value. In Part 2 we use it to call model deployments, diagnose 404 errors, resolve two-instance confusion, and fix resource group provisioning failures.

If your response contains access_token — authentication is complete. Every error in this article is behind you. Part 2 covers everything from this point to a working model response.

entry.title: SAP AI Core: Deployment and Configuration Errors and How to Fix Them (Part 2 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TROUBLESHOOTING  |  SAP AI CORE  |  PART 2 OF 2

SAP AI Core: Deployment and Configuration Errors and How to Fix Them

Every error between a working bearer token and a working model response — 404 on inference calls, two AI Core instance confusion, orchestration vs foundation-models mismatch, resource group provisioning failures, and 409 on delete. Part 2 of 2.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP AI Core Troubleshooting Series  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 1 covered getting your bearer token working. This part covers everything after that — why your inference call returns 404 even though the deployment shows RUNNING, how two AI Core instances cause invisible failures, why the orchestration scenario requires a different request format, and what to do when resource group creation fails with 500 or gets stuck in Provisioning. These errors are harder to diagnose because the symptoms are misleading and the causes are architectural, not syntactic.

Starting Point for This Article

This article assumes Part 1 is complete — you have a working bearer token from a successful OAuth call. The errors here all occur after authentication is working. If you're still hitting 401 or token issues, start with Part 1.

The six errors in this article follow the same sequence most practitioners hit: successfully authenticated, deployment appears to exist, but inference calls fail. Each error reveals a layer of the AI Core architecture that isn't obvious from the documentation.

ERROR 7 OF 12  
ERROR 7    404 on inference call — deployment exists in Launchpad    {"error":{"code":"404"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"404","message":"Resource not found"}}

This happens even though you can see the deployment in AI Launchpad with status RUNNING.

What Causes It

Three common causes — all produce the same 404:

  • The deployment belongs to a different AI Core instance than the one your service key authenticates against (covered in detail in Error 😎
  • The token expired between the deployments list call and the inference call — tokens are valid for 3600 seconds (1 hour)
  • The AI-Resource-Group header is missing or pointing to the wrong resource group
The Fix

First — confirm the deployment ID matches exactly. Get a fresh list with a fresh token:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/lm/deployments" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_FRESH_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default"

Find your deployment in the response. Confirm the id field matches the ID in your inference call URL exactly. Then run the inference call immediately with the same fresh token:

curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_DEPLOYMENT_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_FRESH_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}],"max_tokens":100}"

If your deployments list returns the ID but the inference call still returns 404 — the deployment belongs to a different AI Core instance. This is Error 8.

ERROR 8 OF 12  

ERROR 8    Two AI Core instances — wrong service key    404 on RUNNING deployment 
What You See

Your deployment shows RUNNING in AI Launchpad. Your token call succeeds. Your deployments list curl returns results. But the inference call returns 404 — consistently, with every fresh token.

What Causes It

Many SAP BTP accounts have multiple AI Core service instances — created at different times for different teams or projects. AI Launchpad can connect to multiple instances and lets you switch between them using the workspace selector in the header.

The critical issue: your service key belongs to one AI Core instance. Your deployment was created using a different AI Core instance in Launchpad. Your token authenticates successfully against your instance — but that instance has no record of the deployment that lives in the other instance. The result is a 404 that looks identical to a missing deployment.

How to Diagnose It

Three checks that identify this issue in under two minutes:

  1. Check your service key — the AI_API_URL field identifies which instance your credentials belong to
  2. In AI Launchpad — check the workspace header. It shows which AI Core connection is currently active. Note the name.
  3. Check the resource group labels — in AI Launchpad → Resource Groups → edit the default group → look at the instance_id label. This shows which AI Core instance owns the resource group.

If the instance_id in the resource group labels does not match the instance your service key belongs to — you have the two-instance problem.

The Fix

Two options depending on your access:

  • Option 1 (fastest): Get a service key for the AI Core instance that owns the resource group containing your deployment. BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → find the correct instance → Service Keys → Create. Use the new credentials for token generation and all API calls.
  • Option 2 (self-contained): Create a new resource group in your own AI Core instance, create a new configuration and deployment there, and use your existing service key throughout. This requires your service plan to support additional resource group creation — see Error 9 if this fails.

The instance_id label in the resource group is the fastest way to identify this issue. It directly tells you which AI Core instance the resource group belongs to — compare it against the instance identifier in your service key URL.

ERROR 9 OF 12  
ERROR 9    Resource group stuck in Provisioning    500 on creation / status never changes 
What You See

You create a new resource group via AI Launchpad or curl PUT. The creation returns a 500 error or appears to succeed but the status shows Provisioning and never changes — even after 10+ minutes.

What Causes It

Two common causes:

  • Your AI Core service plan does not support creating additional resource groups. Some SAP internal plans and certain BTP subscription tiers restrict resource group creation to the default group only.
  • The AI Core instance itself has an underlying provisioning issue — a platform-level problem that requires SAP support to resolve.
The Fix

Step 1 — Wait 5 minutes before concluding provisioning has failed. New resource groups typically provision in under 5 minutes. A 10+ minute wait with no status change indicates a failure.

Step 2 — Verify your service plan supports additional resource groups. Check BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → your AI Core instance → plan details. If the plan explicitly restricts resource groups, creating new ones is not possible without upgrading.

Step 3 — If the resource group is stuck, delete it and recreate via curl (more reliable than the UI for initial creation):

# Delete stuck resource group
curl -X DELETE "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/YOUR_GROUP_ID" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"
 
# Recreate via curl
curl -X PUT "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"resourceGroupId": "your-new-group"}"

Step 4 — Check status after 3 minutes:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/your-new-group" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"

If status remains Provisioning after recreation — raise a support ticket with component CA-ML-AIP, include your instance ID and the timestamp of the failed creation attempt.

ERROR 10 OF 12  
ERROR 10    409 on resource group delete    {"error":{"code":"409"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"409","message":"Conflict"}}

This occurs when attempting to delete a resource group that is still in Provisioning state.

What Causes It

HTTP 409 Conflict means the resource is in a state that doesn't allow the requested operation. A resource group in Provisioning state is actively being worked on by the platform — deleting it while provisioning is in progress is not permitted.

The Fix

Wait for the provisioning process to either complete (status: PROVISIONED) or fail (status: ERROR) before attempting to delete. If the status has been Provisioning for over 15 minutes without changing, the platform may be unresponsive — in this case wait 30 minutes before retrying the delete.

If the delete still fails after the status shows ERROR, use the curl DELETE command rather than the UI — the API is more reliable for deleting failed resource groups:

curl -X DELETE "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/YOUR_GROUP_ID" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"
ERROR 11 OF 12  
ERROR 11    Orchestration vs foundation-models scenario mismatch    Empty response or wrong endpoint format 
What You See

Your deployments list returns a deployment with status RUNNING, but when you call the inference endpoint using the standard chat/completions format, you get an empty response or a format error.

What Causes It

SAP AI Core has two distinct deployment scenarios — foundation-models and orchestration. They use different inference endpoint formats and different request body structures.

  • foundation-models deployments use /chat/completions with a standard OpenAI-compatible request body including a model field
  • orchestration deployments use /completion with an orchestration_config wrapper structure — a completely different format

If your deployment was created under the orchestration scenario (check the scenarioId field in the deployments list response) but you're calling it with the foundation-models format, the request either fails silently or returns a format error.

How to Check Your Scenario

In your deployments list response, find the scenarioId field for your deployment:

{"id":"d01e9590...","scenarioId":"orchestration",...}  ← orchestration deployment
{"id":"df600c1a...","scenarioId":"foundation-models",...}  ← foundation-models deployment
The Fix — foundation-models deployment (standard format)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"model":"anthropic--claude-3-5-sonnet","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}],"max_tokens":100}"
The Fix — orchestration deployment (orchestration format)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_ID/completion" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"orchestration_config":{"module_configurations":{"llm_module_config":{"model_name":"anthropic--claude-3-5-sonnet","model_params":{"max_tokens":500}}}},"messages_history":[],"template":{"template":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}}"

For most use cases — direct model access for Python applications and RAG pipelines — the foundation-models scenario is the correct choice. Orchestration is for SAP's managed prompt orchestration service with additional capabilities like content filtering and grounding. If you want direct foundation model access, create your configuration with scenarioId: foundation-models.

ERROR 12 OF 12  
ERROR 12    AI Launchpad showing wrong instance    Configurations visible but inference fails 
What You See

You create a configuration and deployment in AI Launchpad and can see it listed. But when you use curl with your service key credentials, the deployment is either not found in the list or returns 404 on inference.

What Causes It

AI Launchpad is a separate BTP subscription that can connect to multiple AI Core instances. The workspace selector in the Launchpad header controls which instance you're viewing and managing. If you have subscriptions to AI Launchpad in multiple BTP subaccounts, each may open to a different AI Core connection by default.

The result: you create a deployment while connected to instance A in Launchpad, but your service key belongs to instance B. The Launchpad URL in the browser contains the subaccount subdomain — this is the clearest indicator of which Launchpad instance you're using.

How to Diagnose It

Check the browser URL when you're in AI Launchpad. The subdomain in the URL identifies which BTP subaccount (and therefore which AI Launchpad subscription) you're using:

https://YOUR-SUBDOMAIN.ai-launchpad.prod.us-east-1.aws.ai-prod.cloud.sap/...

Compare YOUR-SUBDOMAIN against the subdomain in your service key's url field. If they don't match — you're in the wrong Launchpad.

The Fix
  1. Go to BTP Cockpit → the subaccount that contains your AI Core service instance
  2. Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions → SAP AI Launchpad → Open
  3. This opens the Launchpad connected to that specific subaccount's AI Core instance
  4. Create your configuration and deployment here — it will be visible to your service key credentials

Bookmark the AI Launchpad URL for each subaccount separately. When you have multiple subaccounts, it's easy to open the wrong Launchpad instance by navigating directly to the URL without checking which subaccount it belongs to.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Deployment and Configuration Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

404 on RUNNING deployment

Token expired or wrong resource group header

Refresh token and confirm AI-Resource-Group header matches deployment's group

404 despite correct token and group

Deployment lives in different AI Core instance than service key

Check instance_id label in resource group — get service key for the correct instance

Resource group stuck in Provisioning

Plan restriction or platform-level issue

Wait 5 min, retry via curl, raise CA-ML-AIP ticket if persists beyond 15 min

409 on resource group delete

Resource group still in active Provisioning state

Wait for PROVISIONED or ERROR status before deleting

Empty response or format error on inference

Calling orchestration deployment with foundation-models format

Check scenarioId in deployment list — use /completion endpoint for orchestration

Deployment in Launchpad but not in curl list

Launchpad connected to wrong subaccount's AI Core instance

Open Launchpad from correct BTP subaccount — check browser URL subdomain

THE WORKING INFERENCE CALL 

Complete Working Model Inference Call

Once all twelve errors are resolved, this is the complete working inference call for a foundation-models deployment. All values in capitals are replaced with your actual values:

# Step 1: Get fresh token (valid 1 hour)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"
 
# Step 2: List deployments to confirm ID and resource group
curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/lm/deployments" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default"
 
# Step 3: Call the model
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_DEPLOYMENT_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"model":"YOUR_MODEL_NAME","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello, are you working?"}],"max_tokens":100}"

A successful response contains choices[0].message.content with the model's reply. Your end-to-end connection is proven. From here — move to the generative-ai-hub-sdk for Python integration (covered in the Generative AI Hub series) which handles token management, deployment ID resolution, and model routing automatically.

entry.title: Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the RPT-1 API Token
entry.content:
In my previous blog about experimenting with RPT-1, we explored how to try out using the RPT-1 playground version. Now, let’s see how this can be done programmatically. Please check the  SAP Cloud SDK for Python for RPT-1 native APIs. We will first use request library to call the API directly.

Prerequisites:

  1. An API key for the service – you can get it from the ‘Your API Token’ section at https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs.

 

 

 

We will use the same .csv file used previously for the Freight Orders with ‘[PREDICT]’ tag.

RPT-1 API accept json format. So, lets first convert .csv file to json format.

#Read csv and convert to json
import csv
import json
def csv_to_json(csv_file_path):

    data = []
    with open(csv_file_path, encoding='utf-8') as csv_file:
       reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file) 
        for row in reader:
            data.append(row
    final_payload = {
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": "Carrier",
                    "prediction_placeholder": "[PREDICT]"
                }
            ]
        },
        "index_column": "Document" ,
        "rows": data         
    }
    print(f"Done! {len(data)} records converted to json")
    return final_payload

 

Prepare the request parameters

access_token = "<<Your access token here>>" #Use .env for secure storage
url = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"

Now call the API

import requests
import json
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}
final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv')
response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=final_payload, timeout=120)
response.raise_for_status()
data_pred = response.json()
preds = data_pred.get("prediction", [])
print(json.dumps(preds, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))

The result would look like this.

{
  "id": "ba15b9bd-8cde-4eee-b858-0329f1b48e11",
  "metadata": {
    "num_columns": 38,
    "num_predictions": 1,
    "num_query_rows": 1,
    "num_rows": 334
  },
  "predictions": [
    {
      "Carrier": [
        {
          "confidence": 0.46,
          "confidence_interval": null,
          "prediction": "MM_CAR_01"
        }
      ],
      "Document": 6100000600
    }
  ],
  "status": {
    "code": 0,
    "message": "ok"
  }
}

If you have RPT-1 deployment in SAP AI Launchpad, then you can use native API call.

import os
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.models import RPTRequest, PredictionConfig, TargetColumn
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.client import RPTClient
#Get the credentials from the AI Core instance in your BTP sub-account
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "<<AICORE_AUTH_URL>>oauth/token"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_ID>>"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET>>"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "<<AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP>>"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "<<AICORE_BASE_URL>>/v2"

final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv', target_columns=["Carrier"])

client = RPTClient()
body = RPTRequest(
            prediction_config=PredictionConfig(
                target_columns=[
                    TargetColumn(name="Carrier", task_type="classification")
                ]),
            rows=final_payload
        )
response = client.predict(body=body, model_name="sap-rpt-1-small")
print(response.predictions)

This example shows using Python, but this can be done in ABAP as well using HTTP request call (such as a save process controller strategy method in SAP TM). 

What you learned

  • How to shape tabular data for SAP RPT‑1 with a clear prediction_config and index_column
  • How to invoke the predict API(using request call and native API) securely and read confidence‑scored outputs
  • We used 'predicting carrier' just as an example and to understand the usage. You can think of any other use cases which require prediction from minimal data set which doesn't require complex classic ML training.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 1 OF 6
SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent

What agentic AI actually means in SAP contexts, when to use an agent vs a skill vs a pipeline, the full agentic SAP stack with LangGraph and AI Core, and a working ReAct agent for SAP data — all in Part 1.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 1 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI is not just a more capable pipeline. It's a fundamentally different execution model — the agent decides what to do next based on what it observes, rather than following a fixed sequence. This article covers what that means for SAP workloads specifically, when agents are the right choice vs skills and pipelines, the complete LangGraph + AI Core architecture for production SAP agents, and a working ReAct agent that queries SAP data, analyzes it, and produces findings autonomously.

Series 2 Context — Building on the Generative AI Hub Foundation

This is Part 1 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice — the second series building on the Generative AI Hub foundation. Series 1 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, RAG on HANA Cloud, CAP applications, and Joule Studio. If you haven't read that series, the architecture knowledge from Parts 3–5 will be particularly useful background for this one.

Series 2 moves from pipelines to agents. The distinction matters — and Part 1 establishes it clearly before writing a single line of agent code. The four SAP use cases this series builds toward are: data migration landscape assessment, exception log triage, master data quality, and S/4HANA readiness. Each becomes a specialized agent. By Part 6 they work together as a coordinated multi-agent system.

PART A: CONCEPTS — WHAT AGENTIC AI ACTUALLY MEANS 

Agent vs Skill vs Pipeline — The Distinction That Matters

These three terms are used interchangeably in most SAP AI conversations. They shouldn't be. They represent fundamentally different execution models with different appropriate use cases.

Type

How It Works

SAP Example

Prompt Pipeline

Fixed sequence of prompts — output of one feeds the next. You define every step upfront. The model executes but doesn't decide.

Exception log triage with defined classify → summarize → recommend steps. Predictable, auditable, fast.

Joule Skill

Structured, deterministic capability triggered by user intent. Calls a specific API action. No reasoning or planning involved.

Answering a knowledge base question, looking up a material, creating a badge. Single-purpose, reliable.

AI Agent

Autonomous reasoner. Given a goal, the agent decides which tools to use, in what order, based on what it observes at each step. The sequence is not predetermined.

Assess an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness — the agent decides which systems to query, what to look for, and how to structure findings based on what it discovers.

The key differentiator is decision-making under uncertainty. Pipelines and skills are deterministic — given the same input, they always follow the same path. Agents are adaptive — they observe, reason, and decide what to do next based on what they find.

When to Use Each

Use a pipeline when the steps are known upfront and the output structure is fixed. Use a skill when the task is focused and the API call is well-defined. Use an agent when the task requires exploration, multi-step reasoning, or when the path to completion depends on what the agent discovers along the way.

Scenario

Use

SAP Example

Known steps, fixed output structure

Prompt pipeline

Exception log triage, document extraction

Single focused task, defined API

Joule skill

Material lookup, KB question, badge creation

Goal-directed, path unknown upfront

Agent

Landscape assessment, readiness analysis

Multi-step with dependencies on findings

Agent

Data quality sweep, migration risk scoring

Needs human approval at checkpoints

Agent with human-in-the-loop

Transport risk assessment, SoD conflict review

Agents are not always better than pipelines. They are more expensive (more LLM calls), less predictable (the path varies), and harder to debug. Use agents specifically when the task requires adaptive reasoning. For everything else, pipelines and skills are faster, cheaper, and more reliable.

PART B: ARCHITECTURE — THE AGENTIC SAP STACK 

The Complete LangGraph + AI Core Architecture for SAP Agents

Production SAP agents require four layers working together. LangGraph provides the agent orchestration framework. SAP AI Core provides governed foundation model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. BTP Cloud Foundry provides the deployment and runtime environment.

LangGraph Agent → AI Core (foundation model) → SAP Tools (HANA, APIs) → HANA vector store → Findings output
Why LangGraph for SAP Agents

LangGraph is a graph-based agent orchestration framework built on LangChain. It models agent execution as a directed graph — nodes are actions or decisions, edges are transitions. This architecture makes it uniquely suited for SAP workloads for three reasons:

  • State persistence — LangGraph maintains agent state explicitly across steps. For SAP assessments that query multiple systems and accumulate findings, state persistence is essential.
  • Conditional routing — edges in the graph can be conditional. An agent can take different paths based on what it finds — escalate a critical finding, skip a check if a prerequisite fails, loop back for more data if confidence is low.
  • Human-in-the-loop support — LangGraph has native support for pausing execution and waiting for human approval before proceeding. For SAP processes where autonomous action carries risk, this is not optional.
The ReAct Pattern

ReAct (Reason + Act) is the foundational agent pattern used throughout this series. The agent alternates between two steps:

  • Reason — the model thinks about what it knows and what it needs to do next
  • Act — the model calls a tool (a function, an API, a database query) and observes the result

This cycle continues until the agent has enough information to produce a final answer or reaches a defined stopping condition. For SAP workloads, the tools are SAP-specific: HANA queries, RFC calls, AI Core model invocations, vector store retrievals.

Goal input → Reason: what do I know? → Act: call SAP tool → Observe result → Reason: what next? → Final findings
Agent State Schema for SAP Assessments

Every LangGraph agent requires an explicit state schema. For SAP assessment agents, the state carries the accumulated findings, queries run, systems checked, and confidence scores across all steps:

# Agent state schema — carries context across all reasoning steps

from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class SAPAssessmentState(TypedDict):

    goal:              str          # What the agent is trying to assess

    system_info:       dict         # SAP system metadata collected so far

    findings:          List[dict]   # Accumulated findings with severity

    queries_run:       List[str]    # Queries executed — for audit trail

    tools_used:        List[str]    # Tools called — for transparency

    confidence_score:  float        # Overall confidence in findings

    next_action:       str          # What the agent decided to do next

    human_review_needed: bool       # Flag for human-in-the-loop checkpoint

    final_report:      Optional[str] # Final output when complete
PART C: YOUR FIRST SAP AGENT — REACT WITH LANGGRAPH 

Building a ReAct Agent for SAP Data Assessment

This first agent demonstrates the complete ReAct pattern on a focused SAP task: given a HANA table name, autonomously assess its data quality — checking volume, null rates, duplicate rates, and anomalies — and produce a structured findings report. This is the foundation pattern that the more complex agents in Parts 2–6 build on.

Step 1 — Install Dependencies
# Install LangGraph and SAP AI SDK
pip install langgraph langchain-core generative-ai-hub-sdk hdbcli
Step 2 — Define SAP Tools

Tools are the functions the agent can call. Each tool has a name, description (used by the model to decide when to call it), and implementation. For SAP agents, tools wrap HANA queries and AI Core calls:

# tools.py — SAP tools available to the agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json
 
# HANA connection — credentials from environment or .aicore-config.json
def get_hana_connection():
    return hdb.connect(
        address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'],
        port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'],
        encrypt=True
    )
 
@tool
def get_table_volume(table_name: str) -> str:
    """Get the row count and basic metadata for an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table_name}')
    count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
    return json.dumps({'table': table_name, 'row_count': count})
 
@tool
def get_null_rate(table_name: str, column_name: str) -> str:
    """Calculate the null rate for a specific column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            COUNT(*) as total,
            SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as nulls,
            ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
                / COUNT(*), 2) as null_rate_pct
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'column': column_name,
        'total': row[0], 'nulls': row[1], 'null_rate_pct': float(row[2])
    })
 
@tool
def get_duplicate_rate(table_name: str, key_column: str) -> str:
    """Identify duplicate records based on a key column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT {key_column}) as duplicate_count,
               COUNT(*) as total_count
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'key_column': key_column,
        'duplicate_count': row[0], 'total_count': row[1],
        'duplicate_rate_pct': round(100.0 * row[0] / row[1], 2) if row[1] > 0 else 0
    })
 
@tool
def generate_assessment_report(findings: str) -> str:
    """Generate a structured data quality assessment report from accumulated findings."""
    from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
    llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
    response = llm([
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP data quality expert.
        Generate a structured assessment report from the findings provided.
        Include: Executive Summary, Critical Issues, Recommendations.
        Be specific and actionable.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from findings:\n{findings}')
    ])
    return response.content

Step 3 — Build the LangGraph Agent

# agent.py — LangGraph ReAct agent for SAP data quality assessment
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools import get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report
 
# Initialize foundation model via SAP AI Core
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Register SAP tools the agent can use
tools = [get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report]
 
# System prompt — defines agent behavior and boundaries
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP data quality assessment agent.
Your goal is to autonomously assess the data quality of SAP HANA tables.

For each assessment:
1. Check the table volume first
2. Check null rates for key columns
3. Check duplicate rates on primary key columns
4. Flag any issue where null_rate > 5% or duplicate_rate > 0.1% as HIGH severity
5. Generate a structured findings report when assessment is complete

Always explain your reasoning before calling a tool.
Always summarize what you found after each tool call.
Stop when you have assessed all requested dimensions.'''
 
# Create the ReAct agent — LangGraph handles the reason/act loop
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Step 4 — Run the Agent

# Run the agent on an SAP table assessment task
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the data quality of table MARA (SAP material master).
        Check volume, null rate on MTART and MATKL columns,
        and duplicate rate on MATNR. Generate a findings report.
    ''')]
})
 
# Print the agent's final response
print(result['messages'][-1].content)

The agent will reason through the task step by step — calling get_table_volume first, then get_null_rate for each column, then get_duplicate_rate, and finally generate_assessment_report with all accumulated findings. The reasoning trace is visible in the messages — each HumanMessage and AIMessage shows exactly what the agent thought and did.

What the Agent Output Looks Like

The agent produces a structured findings report with:

  • Executive Summary — overall data quality score and critical issues count
  • Findings per dimension — volume, nulls, duplicates with actual values
  • Severity classification — Critical, High, Medium, Low per finding
  • Specific recommendations — actionable fixes referencing actual column names and values

This output is the foundation for the migration assessment agent in Part 2, which extends this pattern across multiple SAP tables and produces a full landscape readiness report.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT AGENT — EXECUTION TRACE      ║
║         Table: MARA (Material Master — General Data)             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

[THOUGHT] I need to begin by checking the volume of the MARA table to
understand the scale of the assessment before evaluating data quality
dimensions.

[ACTION] get_table_volume("MARA")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "row_count": 847293}

[THOUGHT] The table contains 847,293 material master records. This is
a significant volume — quality issues even at low percentages will
affect thousands of records. I'll now check null rates starting with
MTART (Material Type), a critical classification field.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MTART")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MTART", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 169, "null_rate_pct": 0.02}

[THOUGHT] MTART null rate is 0.02% — acceptable. 169 records without
a material type is low risk. Moving to MATKL (Material Group), which
drives procurement categorization and reporting.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MATKL")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MATKL", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 73,969, "null_rate_pct": 8.73}

[THOUGHT] MATKL null rate is 8.73% — this exceeds the 5% threshold
and is flagged HIGH severity. 73,969 materials without a material group
will cause issues in procurement reporting, spend analytics, and any
AI classification pipeline that relies on MATKL for categorization.
This needs to be highlighted in the final report.

[ACTION] get_duplicate_rate("MARA", "MATNR")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "key_column": "MATNR",
               "duplicate_count": 0, "total_count": 847293,
               "duplicate_rate_pct": 0.0}

[THOUGHT] MATNR (Material Number) has zero duplicates — primary key
integrity is intact. I now have sufficient findings to generate the
structured assessment report.

[ACTION] generate_assessment_report(findings)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT REPORT
Table: MARA — Material Master General Data
Assessment Date: 2026-03-28 | Agent: SAP Data Quality Agent v1.0
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
─────────────────
Overall Data Quality Score: 74/100 — ATTENTION REQUIRED
Records Assessed: 847,293
Critical Issues: 0  |  High Severity: 1  |  Low Severity: 2

One high-severity data quality issue was identified that will impact
procurement reporting, spend analytics, and downstream AI pipelines
that rely on material group classification. Immediate remediation is
recommended before any S/4HANA migration or AI workload deployment.

FINDINGS
────────
┌──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
│ Dimension    │ Column       │ Value         │ Threshold│ Severity │
├──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Volume       │ —            │ 847,293 rows  │ —        │ OK       │
│ Null Rate    │ MTART        │ 0.02% (169)   │ < 5%     │ LOW      │
│ Null Rate    │ MATKL        │ 8.73% (73,969)│ < 5%     │ HIGH ⚠   │
│ Duplicates   │ MATNR        │ 0.0%          │ < 0.1%   │ LOW      │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┴──────────┴──────────┘

HIGH SEVERITY FINDING — MATKL (Material Group) Null Rate: 8.73%
73,969 of 847,293 material records are missing a Material Group
assignment. Impact areas:
  • Procurement spend reporting by category will be incomplete
  • AI-based material classification will produce unreliable results
  • S/4HANA migration data validation will flag these records

RECOMMENDATIONS
───────────────
1. [IMMEDIATE] Run MATKL gap analysis — extract all MATNR records
   where MATKL is null. Group by MTART to identify which material
   types are most affected. This scopes the remediation effort.

2. [SHORT-TERM] Assign Material Groups using the AI classification
   pipeline (Part 2 of Generative AI Hub series) — embed existing
   classified materials as reference examples and classify the
   73,969 unclassified records using RAG-augmented classification.

3. [BEFORE MIGRATION] Validate MATKL completeness reaches < 2%
   null rate before proceeding with any S/4HANA migration data
   load. Include this check in your migration go/no-go criteria.

4. [PROCESS] Add MATKL as a required field in material master
   creation workflows in MM01 to prevent future gaps.

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Agent completed assessment in 4 reasoning steps | 4 tool calls
Confidence Score: 0.91 | Human Review Required: NO
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SERIES 2 ROADMAP 

What This Series Builds Across 6 Parts

 

Part

Title

What It Builds

Part 1

Concepts, Architecture, First ReAct Agent

Agent vs skill vs pipeline, LangGraph + AI Core stack, data quality ReAct agent on HANA

Part 2

SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent

Multi-tool agent that analyzes SAP landscape, scores migration readiness, produces findings report

Part 3

SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

Autonomous root cause analysis — agent queries logs, identifies patterns, recommends actions without human triage

Part 4

SAP Master Data Quality Agent

Detects data issues across master data domains, prioritizes fixes, generates remediation plan

Part 5

S/4HANA Readiness Agent

Assesses custom code and configuration against clean core principles, flags migration blockers

Part 6

Multi-Agent Orchestration

All four agents working together — an orchestrator agent delegates to specialized sub-agents and synthesizes a unified SAP landscape report

What's Next — Part 2

Part 2 builds the SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent — extending the ReAct pattern from a single table to a full landscape assessment across multiple systems, with conditional routing based on findings and a scored readiness report as output. The agent decides autonomously which systems to query, what to look for, and how to weight findings based on migration impact.

If you work through the ReAct agent in this article and want to extend it to your own SAP tables — drop a comment with your use case. The pattern generalizes across any HANA table and any assessment dimension. The tool definitions are the only thing that changes.

entry.title: Your AI Models Just Got a Hardware Upgrade
entry.content:
Co-Author: Lukas Pätz

TL;DR

SAP AI Core (the engine) now supports flexible instance types — pick the exact CPU or GPU your workload needs
New instance types deliver up to 4.5–5x the compute performance of old resource plans
Access the latest GPUs (L4, L40S, H100) without waiting for SAP to package them into plans
Your existing resource plans still work — instance types are opt-in where they help

Start Here: SAP Note 3660109

Why This Matters

If you're running your custom AI models on SAP's Generative AI Hub (technical service SAP AI Core), this one's for you.

We've heard the feedback: newer GPUs are available from hyperscalers, but you can't use them yet because SAP hasn't released a matching resource plan. That lag is frustrating — especially when the difference between a V100 and an H100 isn't incremental. It's generational. We're talking 4.5–5x the compute performance for the right workloads. An inference call that takes 8 seconds on older hardware might finish in under 1 second on newer chips. That's the gap between "possible in theory" and "shipping in production."

Starting now, that gap is closing.
 

What Changed: Resource Plans → Instance Types

Think of the old model like a set menu at a restaurant. Curated, predictable, but limited to what the chef decided to offer.

Instance types are the shift to à la carte. You see the full range of CPUs and GPUs available across cloud providers, and you pick exactly what your workload needs — from a lightweight CPU for preprocessing to an H100 for training large models.

Resource plans aren't going away. They still work. But instance types are now the primary path for getting the newest, most capable hardware into your deployments — faster, and with finer control than the old model allowed. We highly recommend to leverage instance types going forward (only available in the "extended" service plan, now be the right time to migrate from standard to extended)

Here's the point: you tell us what your model needs; we make sure the right compute is there. Bon appétit.

What You Actually Get

A quick clarification: If you're using pre-integrated foundation models through SAP's Generative AI Hub — GPT-5.2, Claude 4.6 Opus or Gemini — this update doesn't directly affect you. Those models are already managed and optimized; you just call the API.

Instance types matter when you're running your own models: custom fine-tuned models, your proprietary models (like team-built solutions), embedding models for RAG pipelines, or specialized computer vision and industry models. That's when hardware choice becomes your concern — and when better hardware translates directly into better outcomes.

Faster inference, faster training

This isn't about spec sheets. It's about what becomes possible with the models you've built.

Custom models that were borderline viable for real-time use — domain-specific classifiers, fine-tuned embedding models, proprietary prediction models — become clearly viable when you can run them on L40S instead of T4. Training jobs that took days can finish in hours on H100. Features your team considered out of reach might now fit inside your existing budget.

L4 and L40S chips deliver substantially higher throughput than T4, at lower cost per inference call. H100 provides order-of-magnitude gains over V100 for training workloads. If you're pushing the limits of what's feasible with custom models, this matters.

Better cost efficiency — without re-architecting

Faster hardware means less compute time per job, which means lower cost. Simple as that.

Instance types also let you right-size. You stop paying for a resource plan heavier than you need. The result: better cost-per-output without touching your model or application logic.

 

New hardware when it ships — not months later

When hyperscalers release the next GPU generation, you won't be waiting for SAP to define a new resource plan. Instance types let us surface new hardware significantly faster. Your infrastructure stays current.

Multi-cloud, multi-region resilience

Instance types are available across cloud providers and regions. Deploy closer to your users or data. Meet compliance requirements. Reduce single-vendor dependency. Keep two or three validated alternatives per region and you're protected against capacity constraints.

Which Instance Should You Use?

 

WorkloadRecommended InstanceExamples
Orchestration, preprocessing, CPU-based MLCPU (AWS m7i, Azure Dv6, GCP n4)Data pipelines, API routing, lightweight model serving
Custom model inference (high throughput)L4 / L40S GPU (AWS g6, g6e)Fine-tuned classifiers, embedding models, custom vision models
Fine-tuning and training medium-to-large modelsA100 (GCP A2 Ultra)Domain-specific model fine-tuning, complex custom inference
Training large language modelsH100 (Azure NC H100 v5)Custom LLM training, proprietary foundation model development

Match the instance to your workload. Account for regional availability. Keep at least two alternatives validated per region.

Use the compute factors in SAP Note 3660109 or the SAP Cost Calculator to compare options on a consistent basis — each instance type maps to a compute factor in node-hours, which makes cost planning straightforward.

Migration: No Big Bang Required

Both options work today. You don't have to migrate everything at once. More details:

# This still works
ai.sap.com/resourcePlan: "infer.s"

# This is the new option — use where it helps
ai.sap.com/instanceType: "g6.4xlarge"

Start with the workloads where newer hardware will have the clearest impact — typically your highest-volume inference jobs or your most compute-heavy training runs. Everything else can stay on resource plans, but newer hardware might be more efficient.

Full specs, regional availability, and compute factors: SAP Note 3660109.

Staying Current

Hardware in AI is moving fast — faster than enterprise software typically keeps up. Instance types are how we keep you on the right side of that curve, without adding operational overhead to your team.

To stay informed:

  • Subscribe to SAP Note 3660109 — it's the live catalogue; new instance types show up here as they become available
  • Watch the What's New page for implementation guidance and new instance families

The Bottom Line

This isn't a small tweak. It's a deliberate shift to put more control in your hands — and to get it there faster.

We handle the complexity: evaluating new hardware, qualifying it, making it available at scale. You focus on what you're actually here to do: build AI solutions that matter.
